# Module Experiment 2: Centre Lathe Operations — Core Concepts

## 1.1 Definition of a Centre Lathe (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Centre Lathe** is a basic machine tool used in the mechanical manufacturing industry for performing a wide range of metal-removal operations by rotating the workpiece about a fixed axis against a single-point cutting tool. The workpiece is held between two rigid supports called the **headstock centre** and the **tailstock centre**, while the tool is traversed in a controlled manner to produce surfaces of revolution (cylindrical, conical, or contoured).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Lab Syllabus Definition (Verbatim):** "A centre lathe is a machine tool in which the workpiece is rotated against a stationary cutting tool; the tool is fed against the rotating workpiece to produce cylindrical and other surfaces of revolution."

The lathe is the father of all machine tools — virtually every other machining process (milling, grinding, boring) is derived from it.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Modernised Potter's Wheel"

Imagine a **potter's wheel**. The potter places a lump of clay on a spinning wheel and uses his fingers to *shape* the outer profile. The clay rotates, and the shaping tool (his hand) is fed slowly across the profile.

The **centre lathe** works on the exact same principle:
- The **clay** is your **raw workpiece** (mild steel, aluminium, brass, etc.)
- The **spinning wheel** is the **headstock spindle** of the lathe
- The **potter's hand** is the **single-point cutting tool** held in the tool post
- The **slow inward movement of the hand** corresponds to the **cross-slide feed**
- The **left-to-right movement** corresponds to the **longitudinal feed** of the carriage

This analogy is particularly useful when introducing *form turning* and *contour turning* — the tool profile is literally the negative of the desired final shape.

## 1.3 Quick Overview of All Operations in This Experiment

| # | Operation | Primary Purpose | Typical Tool Used |
|---|-----------|----------------|--------------------|
| 1 | **Facing** | Producing a flat surface perpendicular to the axis | Facing tool (HSS or Carbide) |
| 2 | **Plain Turning** | Reducing the diameter of a cylindrical workpiece | Straight turning tool |
| 3 | **Step Turning** | Producing multiple diameters on a single shaft | Straight turning tool (multiple passes) |
| 4 | **Taper Turning** | Producing a conical (tapered) surface | Taper turning attachment / compound slide |
| 5 | **Form Turning** | Producing a contoured profile in one pass | Form tool (profile ground) |
| 6 | **Groove Cutting** | Cutting narrow channels for snap-rings, oil seals, etc. | Parting / grooving tool |
| 7 | **Knurling** | Producing a diamond/straight pattern for grip | Knurling tool (two hardened rollers) |
| 8 | **Chamfering** | Bevelling the edge of a workpiece | Chamfer tool (45° point) |
| 9 | **Multi-Start Thread** | Cutting threads with more than one helix | Threading tool + indexing |
| 10 | **Square Thread** | Cutting power-transmission threads with 90° flank | Square-thread tool (flat profile) |
| 11 | **Internal Thread** | Cutting threads inside a pre-bored hole | Internal threading bar / boring bar with tool bit |
| 12 | **Inspection** | Verifying dimensional and geometric accuracy | Vernier, micrometer, thread gauge, plug gauge |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this experiment is central to KTU PCRUL407:** It integrates *machine setup*, *cutting-parameter selection*, *tool geometry*, and *metrology* in a single session — directly satisfying Course Outcomes **CO1** (Identify and operate basic machine tools) and **CO2** (Apply cutting parameters for conventional machining).

## 1.4 Standard Cutting Parameters (Industrial Defaults)

The following baseline values are universally accepted in the KTU-approved workshop manual:

- **Cutting Speed (V):** **30 m/min** for Mild Steel, **60 m/min** for Aluminium, **25 m/min** for Cast Iron
- **Feed (f):** **0.1–0.3 mm/rev** for finishing, **0.3–0.8 mm/rev** for roughing
- **Depth of Cut (d):** **0.5–1.0 mm** for finishing, **2.0–5.0 mm** for roughing
- **Coolant:** Soluble oil + water (1:20 ratio) for steel; dry cutting for cast iron

## 1.5 Safety & Standard Operating Procedure (SOP)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Lab Safety Mandate:** Failure to follow SOPs is the #1 cause of marks being deducted in the continuous evaluation (CE) component.

1. Wear **safety goggles, apron, and closed-toe shoes** (no loose clothing, no gloves near spindles).
2. Ensure the **workpiece is securely mounted** between centres or in a chuck.
3. **Set the lowest spindle speed** for the first cut and increase gradually.
4. **Engage the half-nut (threading dial)** only when the tool is at the start position.
5. **Never leave the chuck key** in the chuck after tightening.
6. Stop the spindle fully before applying the **thread gauge** to the workpiece.
7. Always use a **chip hook or brush** to clear swarf — never use bare hands.

# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Cutting-Parameter Formulas

Every lathe calculation begins with three inter-related primary quantities: **Spindle Speed (N)**, **Cutting Speed (V)**, and **Feed (f)**. These formulas are non-negotiable for KTU university examinations.

### 2.1.1 Spindle Speed (N) — in revolutions per minute (rpm)

The spindle speed depends directly on the diameter being machined and the cutting speed recommended for the workpiece material. As the diameter reduces (after a turning pass), the spindle speed must be increased to keep cutting speed constant.

$$
N = \frac{1000 \times V}{\pi \times D}
$$

Where:
- $N$ = Spindle speed in **rpm** (revolutions per minute)
- $V$ = Cutting speed in **m/min**
- $D$ = Diameter of the workpiece in **mm** (the uncut surface)

### 2.1.2 Material Removal Rate (MRR)

For a turning operation, MRR is the volume of metal removed per unit time.

$$
\text{MRR} = \pi \times D \times d \times f \times N \times 10^{-3} \quad \text{(cm}^3\text{/min)}
$$

Or more elegantly expressed using cutting speed:

$$
\text{MRR} = 1000 \times V \times d \times f \quad \text{(mm}^3\text{/s)}
$$

### 2.1.3 Power Required at the Spindle

$$
P = \frac{\text{MRR} \times K_c}{60 \times 10^6 \times \eta}
$$

Where:
- $P$ = Power in **kW**
- $K_c$ = Specific cutting energy in **N/mm²** (≈ 2000 N/mm² for mild steel, 800 N/mm² for aluminium)
- $\eta$ = Mechanical efficiency of the lathe (typically **0.7 to 0.85**)

## 2.2 Taper Turning Formulas

Taper is expressed in four standard ways. The KTU lab manual uses **Taper per inch** and **Taper angle**.

### 2.2.1 Taper per inch (TPI in metric equivalent)

$$
T = \frac{D - d}{L}
$$

Where:
- $T$ = Taper ratio (dimensionless)
- $D$ = Large diameter in **mm**
- $d$ = Small diameter in **mm**
- $L$ = Length of taper in **mm**

### 2.2.2 Taper Angle (α/2) — Half-Included Angle

$$
\tan\!\left(\frac{\alpha}{2}\right) = \frac{D - d}{2L}
$$

### 2.2.3 Set-Over Method (Tailstock Offset)

When the tailstock is offset by a distance $x$, the resulting taper is:

$$
x = \frac{L \times (D - d)}{2 \times l}
$$

Where:
- $x$ = Tailstock offset in **mm**
- $l$ = Total length of the workpiece in **mm**
- $L$ = Length of the tapered section in **mm**

### 2.2.4 Compound Slide Method

The compound slide is swivelled to an angle $\theta$ measured from the longitudinal axis. The required setting is:

$$
\theta = \frac{\alpha}{2} = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{D - d}{2L}\right)
$$

## 2.3 Thread Cutting Formulas (Multi-Start, Square, Internal)

### 2.3.1 General Thread Geometry

For a **single-start thread**:
- **Pitch (P)** = axial distance per thread (mm)
- **Lead (L)** = axial advance per revolution (mm)
- **Depth of V-thread (h)** = **0.6134 × P** (for ISO metric thread, theoretical)
- **Major diameter (d)** = nominal size
- **Minor diameter (d₁)** = d − 2h
- **Thread angle** = **60°** (V-thread), **29°** (Whitworth), **90°** (Square)

### 2.3.2 Multi-Start Thread Formula

A **multi-start thread** has $n$ parallel helical grooves cut simultaneously. The defining relationship is:

$$
\text{Lead} \; L = n \times P
$$

Where:
- $L$ = Lead (axial advance per revolution) in **mm**
- $n$ = Number of thread starts (1, 2, 3, 4…)
- $P$ = Pitch (axial distance between adjacent threads) in **mm**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Indexing:** After cutting the first start, the tool is retracted, the spindle is indexed by **360°/n**, and the second start is cut in the same helical groove path. For a **4-start thread**, the indexing increment is **90°**.

### 2.3.3 Square Thread Geometry

Square threads are used in **power transmission** (screw jacks, lead screws) because of their high efficiency (up to **90%**).

$$
\text{Depth of square thread} \; h = \frac{P}{2}
$$

$$
\text{Width of thread at root} = P - \text{clearance} \; (\approx 0.05 \text{ to } 0.1 \text{ mm per side})
$$

### 2.3.4 Internal Thread (Tapping on a Lathe)

For an internal metric thread, the **minor (tap drill) diameter** is:

$$
d_1 = d - P
$$

Where:
- $d_1$ = Tap drill (minor) diameter
- $d$ = Nominal (major) thread diameter
- $P$ = Pitch

## 2.4 Knurling — Pitch and Roller Selection

Knurling creates a diamond or straight pattern. The pitch of the knurl depends on the **diametral pitch (DP)** of the knurling rollers and the **workpiece diameter**.

$$
\text{Pitch of knurl} = \frac{\pi \times D}{T}
$$

Where:
- $D$ = Workpiece diameter in **mm**
- $T$ = Total number of teeth on the rollers for a full revolution
- Standard roller pitches: **0.5, 0.75, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0 mm** (DP)

## 2.5 Inspection Formulas

### 2.5.1 Least Count of Vernier Calliper

$$
\text{LC} = \frac{\text{Smallest main scale division}}{\text{Number of vernier divisions}}
$$

### 2.5.2 Least Count of Micrometer

For a standard metric micrometer with **0.5 mm pitch screw** and **50 thimble divisions**:

$$
\text{LC} = \frac{0.5 \text{ mm}}{50} = 0.01 \text{ mm}
$$

## 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Master Table)

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **single most important table** to memorise for the ESE. Every line has appeared (or will appear) in a past KTU paper.

| Parameter | Formula | Units | Application |
|------------|---------|-------|--------------|
| Spindle Speed | $N = \frac{1000 \times V}{\pi \times D}$ | rpm | All turning operations |
| Feed Rate | $f_r = f \times N$ | mm/min | Carriage traverse rate |
| MRR (Turning) | $\text{MRR} = \pi \times D \times d \times f \times N \times 10^{-3}$ | cm³/min | Productivity analysis |
| Taper Ratio | $T = \frac{D - d}{L}$ | dimensionless | Taper turning |
| Half-Angle | $\tan(\alpha/2) = \frac{D - d}{2L}$ | degrees | Taper turning |
| Tailstock Offset | $x = \frac{L(D - d)}{2l}$ | mm | Set-over method |
| Multi-Start Lead | $L = n \times P$ | mm | Multi-start threads |
| V-Thread Depth | $h = 0.6134 \times P$ | mm | V-threads |
| Square Thread Depth | $h = P/2$ | mm | Square threads |
| Internal Tap Drill | $d_1 = d - P$ | mm | Internal threads |
| Knurl Pitch | $P_k = \frac{\pi D}{T}$ | mm | Knurling |
| Cutting Power | $P = \frac{\text{MRR} \times K_c}{60 \times 10^6 \times \eta}$ | kW | Motor sizing |
| Cutting Force | $F_c = K_c \times d \times f$ | N | Force analysis |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Facing** is the foundation of every lathe job — no cylindrical part is usable without a flat reference face.
- **Plain turning** is the workhorse of shaft manufacturing for pumps, motors, and gear blanks.
- **Taper turning** is critical for **Morse tapers** (drill shanks), **Brown & Sharpe tapers**, and **pipe threading adapters**.
- **Multi-start threads** are used in **lead screws of CNC machines** for rapid traverse (faster linear motion per revolution).
- **Square threads** dominate **screw jacks and clamping devices** because of their **self-locking behaviour** and high efficiency.
- **Knurling** is mandatory on **knobs, instrument handles, and thimbles** for tactile grip.
- **Internal threads** are the basis of every **bolt-hole, nut, and coupling** in mechanical assemblies.

# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Spindle Speed Formula

**Given:** A mild steel workpiece of diameter $D = 60$ mm is to be turned at a recommended cutting speed $V = 30$ m/min.

**Step 1:** Understand the definition. Cutting speed is the linear velocity at which the cutting edge engages the workpiece surface.

$$
V = \pi \times D \times N \quad \text{(with D in metres and N in rev/s)}
$$

**Step 2:** Convert all units to be consistent. The KTU standard uses $D$ in **mm**, $V$ in **m/min**, and $N$ in **rev/min (rpm)**. Convert mm to m by dividing by 1000:

$$
V \; (\text{m/min}) = \pi \times \frac{D}{1000} \times N
$$

**Step 3:** Solve for $N$ algebraically:

$$
N = \frac{V \times 1000}{\pi \times D}
$$

**Step 4:** Substitute the numerical values:

$$
N = \frac{30 \times 1000}{\pi \times 60} = \frac{30000}{188.496} \approx 159.15 \text{ rpm}
$$

**Step 5:** Round to the nearest spindle-speed step on the lathe (typically 160 rpm for a 9-speed cone-pulley lathe, or an electronic equivalent). Always round **down** rather than up to avoid exceeding the recommended cutting speed.

> **Result:** $N \approx 160$ rpm for the first pass.

## 3.2 Derivation of Taper Angle from Set-Over Method

**Given:** A workpiece of total length $l = 300$ mm has a tapered section of length $L = 100$ mm. The large diameter is $D = 50$ mm and the small diameter is $d = 40$ mm.

**Step 1:** The geometry of set-over. When the tailstock is moved sideways by a distance $x$, the axis of the workpiece makes a small angle $\phi$ with the lathe axis. The relationship is:

$$
\tan \phi = \frac{x}{l}
$$

**Step 2:** The taper produced is twice this angle (since the offset affects both ends):

$$
\alpha = 2\phi
$$

**Step 3:** From the taper definition, the half-angle is:

$$
\tan \phi = \tan\!\left(\frac{\alpha}{2}\right) = \frac{D - d}{2L}
$$

**Step 4:** Equate the two expressions for $\tan \phi$:

$$
\frac{x}{l} = \frac{D - d}{2L}
$$

**Step 5:** Solve for the required offset $x$:

$$
x = \frac{l \times (D - d)}{2L}
$$

**Step 6:** Substitute the numerical values:

$$
x = \frac{300 \times (50 - 40)}{2 \times 100} = \frac{300 \times 10}{200} = \frac{3000}{200} = 15 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 7:** Verify by computing the half-angle:

$$
\tan\!\left(\frac{\alpha}{2}\right) = \frac{10}{200} = 0.05
$$

$$
\frac{\alpha}{2} = \tan^{-1}(0.05) \approx 2.86^{\circ}
$$

$$
\alpha \approx 5.72^{\circ}
$$

> **Result:** Tailstock offset $x = 15$ mm; included taper angle $\alpha \approx 5.72^{\circ}$.

## 3.3 Derivation of Multi-Start Thread Geometry

**Given:** A 4-start square thread on a screw of nominal diameter $d = 40$ mm, pitch $P = 6$ mm.

**Step 1:** Identify what "4-start" means. Four parallel helical grooves are cut, all starting 90° apart on the circumference. The tool must therefore advance axially by 4 thread pitches per revolution.

**Step 2:** Apply the multi-start relationship:

$$
\text{Lead} \; L = n \times P = 4 \times 6 = 24 \text{ mm/rev}
$$

**Step 3:** The axial distance between adjacent starts (which is the **pitch** of the multi-start thread) is:

$$
P = \frac{L}{n} = \frac{24}{4} = 6 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4:** For a **square thread**, the depth is half the pitch:

$$
h = \frac{P}{2} = \frac{6}{2} = 3 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 5:** The major diameter (outside diameter of the thread) is the nominal diameter of the screw blank:

$$
d_{\text{major}} = 40 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 6:** The minor diameter (root diameter) is:

$$
d_{\text{minor}} = d_{\text{major}} - 2h = 40 - 2(3) = 34 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 7:** Verify the lead using the threading gear-train ratio. On a typical KTU workshop lathe with a 4-mm leadscrew and a 1:6 change-gear pair for a 1.5-mm pitch, the operator must swap to a 4:6 or 1:1.5 ratio (depending on the leadscrew pitch). The lead is then:

$$
L = P_{\text{leadscrew}} \times \frac{N_{\text{driver}}}{N_{\text{driven}}}
$$

For a 4-mm leadscrew and a 4:6 ratio:

$$
L = 4 \times \frac{4}{6} = 2.67 \text{ mm/rev}
$$

This would require **multiple indexing passes** (each pass cuts one start) for a 4-start thread.

> **Result:** Lead $L = 24$ mm/rev; depth $h = 3$ mm; minor diameter $d_{\text{minor}} = 34$ mm.

## 3.4 Derivation of Internal Thread Tap Drill Diameter

**Given:** Cut an M16 × 2.0 internal thread inside a bore.

**Step 1:** Identify the standard ISO metric thread. M16 × 2.0 means major diameter $d = 16$ mm, pitch $P = 2$ mm.

**Step 2:** The depth of a V-thread (theoretical) is:

$$
h = 0.6134 \times P = 0.6134 \times 2 = 1.2268 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3:** The minor diameter is the diameter at the root of the thread:

$$
d_1 = d - 2h = 16 - 2(1.2268) = 13.546 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4:** To make tapping easier and avoid excessive torque, the KTU lab practice is to drill **slightly oversize** (using the **tap drill size**). The empirical rule is:

$$
d_{\text{tap drill}} = d - P = 16 - 2 = 14 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 5:** Choose the nearest standard drill bit from the workshop set. Standard fractional metric drills are available in 0.5-mm increments. Select **14.0 mm** directly.

> **Result:** Tap drill size = **14.0 mm**. This gives a 75% thread engagement, which is the KTU standard.

## 3.5 Worked Example: Full Plain Turning Calculation

**Problem (KTU University Exam — May 2023, Modified):**
A mild steel shaft of diameter 80 mm and length 200 mm is to be reduced to 70 mm diameter over a length of 150 mm. Cutting speed $V = 30$ m/min, feed $f = 0.3$ mm/rev, depth of cut $d = 2.5$ mm. Determine:
1. Spindle speed
2. Material removal rate
3. Time for one pass

**Step 1:** Spindle speed using $D = 80$ mm (initial diameter for the first pass):

$$
N = \frac{1000 \times 30}{\pi \times 80} = \frac{30000}{251.33} = 119.37 \text{ rpm}
$$

Round down to nearest available speed: **$N = 120$ rpm**.

**Step 2:** Material removal rate:

$$
\text{MRR} = \pi \times D \times d \times f \times N \times 10^{-3} \text{ cm}^3\text{/min}
$$

$$
\text{MRR} = \pi \times 80 \times 2.5 \times 0.3 \times 120 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
\text{MRR} = 3.1416 \times 80 \times 2.5 \times 0.3 \times 120 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
\text{MRR} = 3.1416 \times 7200 \times 10^{-3} = 22.62 \text{ cm}^3\text{/min}
$$

**Step 3:** Time for one pass of length $L = 150$ mm. First compute the feed rate in mm/min:

$$
f_r = f \times N = 0.3 \times 120 = 36 \text{ mm/min}
$$

Time for one pass:

$$
t = \frac{L}{f_r} = \frac{150}{36} = 4.17 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 4:** Since the depth of cut is 2.5 mm and the total reduction is 5 mm, **two passes** are required. Total time = $2 \times 4.17 = 8.33$ min (plus tool approach and return time).

> **Result:** $N = 120$ rpm, MRR $= 22.62$ cm³/min, $t_{\text{one pass}} = 4.17$ min.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Operation-wise Tool Geometry

| Operation | Tool Material | Rake Angle | Clearance Angle | Special Feature |
|-----------|---------------|------------|-----------------|-----------------|
| Facing | HSS / Carbide | 5°–15° | 5°–10° | Side cutting edge 0°–15° |
| Plain Turning | HSS | 10°–20° | 6°–10° | Nose radius 0.4–0.8 mm |
| Step Turning | HSS | 10°–20° | 6°–10° | Sharp corner at nose |
| Taper Turning | HSS | 10°–20° | 6°–10° | Side relief grinded to taper angle |
| Form Turning | HSS / Carbide | 0°–10° | 5°–8° | Profile ground to match required shape |
| Groove Cutting | HSS | 0°–5° | 3°–5° | Narrow, ground on both sides |
| Knurling | Hardened Steel Rollers | N/A | N/A | Two rollers, diamond/pattern |
| Chamfering | HSS | 10°–20° | 6°–10° | 45° point angle |
| Threading (V) | HSS | 0°–10° | 5°–10° | Point angle = thread angle |
| Square Thread | HSS | 0° | 3°–5° | Flat tool, 90° profile |
| Internal Thread | HSS / Carbide | 5°–15° | 5°–10° | Mounted on boring bar |

# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Centre Lathe

The following Mermaid block-diagram represents the **functional architecture** of a centre lathe and the interactions between its main sub-systems. This is the standard KTU-lab block diagram used in the ESE viva.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Power Source<br/>Electric Motor 1.5 to 5 HP] --> B[Headstock<br/>Spindle and Gear Train]
    B --> C[Chuck or Face Plate<br/>Workpiece Holding]
    C --> D[Workpiece<br/>Raw Material]
    E[Tailstock<br/>Movable Support] --> D
    B --> F[Feed Gearbox<br/>Power Transmission]
    F --> G[Leadscrew and Feed Rod]
    G --> H[Apron and Carriage]
    H --> I[Cross Slide and Compound Slide]
    I --> J[Tool Post and Cutting Tool]
    J --> K[Workpiece Machined Surface]
    L[Coolant Pump] --> J
    M[Bed and Ways] --> H
    M --> E
```

> **Reading guide:** The main power flow runs from Motor → Headstock → Workpiece (rotation). The feed power flow runs from Spindle → Feed Gearbox → Leadscrew/Feed Rod → Apron → Carriage → Cross Slide → Tool (linear motion). The Tailstock is the counter-support. The Bed and Ways are the structural foundation.

## 4.2 Process Flow for Thread Cutting (Multi-Start Square Thread)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1[Start: Mount workpiece between centres] --> S2[Facing: Flatten end face A]
    S2 --> S3[Plain turn: Reduce to minor diameter over thread length]
    S3 --> S4[Chamfer the thread start: 1 mm x 45 deg]
    S4 --> S5[Set threading tool: depth = pitch divided by 2]
    S5 --> S6[Engage half-nut at tool start position]
    S6 --> S7[Cut first start: full thread length]
    S7 --> S8[Retract tool, disengage half-nut, return carriage]
    S8 --> S9[Index spindle by 360 degrees divided by number of starts]
    S9 --> S10[Engage half-nut at start]
    S10 --> S11{All starts cut?}
    S11 -->|No| S7
    S11 -->|Yes| S12[Final inspection: thread gauge, pitch measurement]
    S12 --> S13[Stop spindle, remove workpiece, deburr]
```

## 4.3 Functional Block Diagram — Thread Geometry Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph VThread[V-Thread ISO Metric]
        V1[Major Diameter d] --> V2[Thread depth h equals 0.6134 times Pitch]
        V2 --> V3[Minor Diameter d1 equals d minus 1.0825 times P]
        V3 --> V4[Thread angle equals 60 degrees]
    end
    subgraph SqThread[Square Thread]
        Q1[Major Diameter d] --> Q2[Thread depth h equals 0.5 times Pitch]
        Q2 --> Q3[Minor Diameter d1 equals d minus Pitch]
        Q3 --> Q4[Thread angle equals 90 degrees]
    end
    subgraph MultiStart[Multi-Start Thread]
        M1[Number of starts n] --> M2[Lead L equals n times Pitch]
        M2 --> M3[Indexing increment equals 360 degrees divided by n]
        M3 --> M4[Pitch P equals L divided by n]
    end
```

## 4.4 Taper Turning Method Selection Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Start[Taper Required] --> Q1{Is taper length greater than 100 mm?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| MethodA[Set-Over Method<br/>Tailstock offset]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Is taper angle small less than 10 degrees?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| MethodB[Compound Slide Method<br/>Swivel slide to half-angle]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{Is precision high and internal taper required?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| MethodC[Taper Turning Attachment<br/>Boring bar / cross-slide guide]
    Q3 -->|No| MethodD[Form Tool Method<br/>for short tapers]
```

## 4.5 Knurling Process Functional Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    K1[Workpiece surface to be knurled] --> K2[Apply coolant heavily]
    K2 --> K3[Mount knurling tool in tool post]
    K3 --> K4[Bring rollers into contact at zero pressure]
    K4 --> K5[Engage automatic feed with high pressure]
    K5 --> K6[Rollers imprint diamond pattern by plastic deformation]
    K6 --> K7[Retract tool at end of pattern length]
    K7 --> K8[Inspect pattern with magnifying glass and feeler gauge]
    K8 --> K9{Quality acceptable?}
    Q9 -->|No| K10[Re-knurl with deeper pressure]
    Q9 -->|Yes| K11[Finished knurled surface]
```
