# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Evaluation Pattern (Verified):** End Semester Exam (ESE) for laboratory courses is a **written practical exam** of **100 marks** with **70 marks** for the written paper and **30 marks** for viva + record. The questions below follow the **PCRUL407 question paper pattern** observed in **Dec 2023 and May 2024** sessions.

## PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Define the term "Taper" in the context of lathe operations. List the three standard methods of producing a taper on a centre lathe.**

**Model Answer:**
Taper is defined as the uniform increase or decrease in the diameter of a workpiece along its length. It is mathematically expressed as the difference between the large diameter and the small diameter divided by the length of the tapered section, i.e. $T = (D - d) / L$.

The three standard methods of taper turning are:
1. **Set-over method** (tailstock offset) — used for small tapers on long shafts.
2. **Compound slide method** — used for short tapers with steep angles; the compound slide is swivelled to the half-angle.
3. **Taper turning attachment method** — uses a linear guide bar attached to the cross-slide; used for internal tapers and high precision.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for the list with one-line descriptions.)*

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**State the difference between Pitch and Lead of a thread. What is the relationship between them for a multi-start thread?**

**Model Answer:**
**Pitch** is defined as the axial distance between two adjacent thread crests. **Lead** is the axial distance the thread advances in one complete revolution of the workpiece.

For a **single-start thread**, Pitch = Lead. For a **multi-start thread** with $n$ starts:

$$
\text{Lead} = n \times \text{Pitch}
$$

For example, in a 4-start thread with a pitch of 6 mm, the lead is 24 mm per revolution.

*(3 marks: 1 for definitions + 2 for the formula with an example.)*

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Rule:** In the ESE, every module question offers an **internal choice** — students must answer **either Option A or Option B**. Both options are full 14-mark questions. Below, **Question A** and **Question B** are independent alternatives.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

**(a)** A mild steel shaft of **80 mm diameter** and **length 250 mm** is to be turned to a **stepped profile** with a **50 mm diameter section of length 100 mm**, a **30 mm diameter section of length 80 mm**, and a **chamfer of 1.5 mm × 45°** at the step junction. Calculate:
1. The cutting speed recommended, the spindle speed, and the feed rate if $V = 30$ m/min and $f = 0.25$ mm/rev. (5 marks)
2. The total machining time assuming two roughing passes and one finishing pass per diameter reduction. (2 marks)

**(b)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **set-over method** of taper turning. A shaft of **length 300 mm** has to be tapered from **60 mm to 50 mm** over a **length of 150 mm**. Determine the **tailstock offset** required. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution for Question A (a)

**Given Data:**
- Initial diameter $D_1 = 80$ mm
- First step diameter $D_2 = 50$ mm
- Second step diameter $D_3 = 30$ mm
- Workpiece length $L_{\text{wp}} = 250$ mm
- Length of 50 mm section: 100 mm
- Length of 30 mm section: 80 mm
- Cutting speed $V = 30$ m/min
- Feed $f = 0.25$ mm/rev

**Step 1 — Spindle speed for the first pass (roughing on the 80 mm diameter):**

$$
N_1 = \frac{1000 \times V}{\pi \times D_1} = \frac{1000 \times 30}{\pi \times 80} = \frac{30000}{251.33} \approx 119.37 \text{ rpm}
$$

Round down to nearest step: **$N_1 = 120$ rpm** (5 marks total break down: 2 for formula, 1 for substitution, 1 for result, 1 for rounding/justification).

**Step 2 — Feed rate:**

$$
f_r = f \times N_1 = 0.25 \times 120 = 30 \text{ mm/min}
$$

**Step 3 — Time calculation:**

- Total diameter reduction from 80 to 50 mm = **30 mm** → depth per pass = 5 mm → **6 roughing passes** (1 finishing pass) at length 100 mm
- Diameter reduction from 50 to 30 mm = **20 mm** → 4 roughing + 1 finishing at length 80 mm

Time for a single pass of length $L$:

$$
t = \frac{L}{f_r}
$$

- For 80→50 mm: 7 passes × $\frac{100}{30}$ = $7 \times 3.33$ = **23.33 min**
- For 50→30 mm: 5 passes × $\frac{80}{30}$ = $5 \times 2.67$ = **13.33 min**

$$
t_{\text{total}} = 23.33 + 13.33 = 36.66 \text{ min} \approx 36.7 \text{ min}
$$

(2 marks for the time calculation with proper units.)

---

#### Model Solution for Question A (b)

**Sketch (ASCII representation, equivalent to KTU expected figure):**

```
         Large Dia (D)              Small Dia (d)
              |  ____________________  |
              | /                    \ |
              |/                      \|
        ======+========================+======   ← Lathe axis
             /|                      |\
            / |                      | \         ← Tapered section
           /  |                      |  \          of length L
          /   |                      |   \
        ====  |______________________|   ====   ← Tailstock offset by x
        ___________________________
            Total workpiece length = l
```

**Explanation of the Set-Over Method (4 marks for theory):**

In the set-over method, the tailstock is bodily shifted sideways (perpendicular to the lathe axis) by a calculated distance $x$. This causes the axis of the workpiece to align along a slightly inclined line, and the cutting tool, which moves along the true lathe axis, gradually cuts a tapered surface. The amount of offset is computed using the formula:

$$
x = \frac{l \times (D - d)}{2 \times L}
$$

**Step-by-Step Calculation (3 marks for computation):**

**Given:**
- Total workpiece length $l = 300$ mm
- Taper length $L = 150$ mm
- Large diameter $D = 60$ mm
- Small diameter $d = 50$ mm

**Step 1:** Compute the diameter difference:

$$
D - d = 60 - 50 = 10 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute into the set-over formula:

$$
x = \frac{300 \times 10}{2 \times 150} = \frac{3000}{300} = 10 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3:** Verify by computing the half-taper angle:

$$
\tan\!\left(\frac{\alpha}{2}\right) = \frac{D - d}{2L} = \frac{10}{2 \times 150} = \frac{10}{300} = 0.0333
$$

$$
\frac{\alpha}{2} = \tan^{-1}(0.0333) \approx 1.91^{\circ}
$$

$$
\alpha \approx 3.82^{\circ}
$$

> **Final Answer:** Tailstock offset $x = 10$ mm; Taper angle $\alpha \approx 3.82^{\circ}$. (KTU 7-mark distribution: 4 for sketch + theory, 3 for calculation.)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2023] — Internal Choice

**(a)** With the help of a diagram, explain the **method of cutting a square thread on a lathe**. A **30 mm diameter** shaft has to be threaded with a **square thread of pitch 5 mm** and **2 starts**. Calculate the:
1. Lead of the thread. (2 marks)
2. Depth of cut. (2 marks)
3. Minor diameter of the thread. (3 marks)

**(b)** Explain the procedure of **knurling** on a centre lathe. State the two standard knurling patterns and list the two materials commonly used for knurling rollers. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution for Question B (a)

**Diagram (KTU expected sketch):**

```
           Top View of Square Thread Profile
       |----Pitch P----|
       |              |
       +--------------+
       |              |  ← Crest (flat top, width P minus clearance)
       |              |
       |              |
       +--------------+  ← Root (90 deg square corners)
       |  Minor Dia   |
       |              |
```

**Calculation:**

**Step 1 — Lead of the thread:**

For a 2-start thread with pitch $P = 5$ mm and $n = 2$:

$$
L = n \times P = 2 \times 5 = 10 \text{ mm/rev}
$$

**Step 2 — Depth of cut for a square thread:**

For a square thread, the depth is half the pitch:

$$
h = \frac{P}{2} = \frac{5}{2} = 2.5 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Minor diameter of the thread:**

The major diameter equals the nominal shaft diameter (since the thread is cut, not rolled): $d_{\text{major}} = 30$ mm.

$$
d_{\text{minor}} = d_{\text{major}} - 2h = 30 - 2(2.5) = 30 - 5 = 25 \text{ mm}
$$

> **Final Answer:** $L = 10$ mm/rev, $h = 2.5$ mm, $d_{\text{minor}} = 25$ mm. (7 marks distributed: 2 for diagram explanation, 5 for three calculations as above.)

---

#### Model Solution for Question B (b)

**Knurling Procedure (4 marks for procedure explanation):**

Knurling is a chipless forming operation in which a pattern of straight, diagonal, or diamond-shaped indentations is impressed on a cylindrical surface using hardened rollers. The procedure is as follows:

1. **Workpiece preparation:** The surface to be knurled must first be **plain turned** to a clean, accurate diameter corresponding to **above the knurl pitch midpoint** (so the final diameter ends up as required after plastic deformation).
2. **Coolant application:** Apply a generous amount of **cutting fluid or soluble oil** to reduce friction and prevent roll wear.
3. **Tool mounting:** Mount the **knurling tool** in the tool post with the rollers aligned with the workpiece axis (for diamond knurl) or perpendicular (for straight knurl).
4. **Initial contact:** Bring the rollers into **light contact** with the workpiece; the rollers should just touch the surface.
5. **Pressure application:** Apply firm cross-slide pressure to force the rollers into the metal. The pattern is formed by **plastic deformation**, not by cutting.
6. **Feed engagement:** Engage the **automatic longitudinal feed**; the rollers rotate and imprint the pattern along the full knurled length.
7. **Retraction:** At the end of the knurled length, **retract the cross-slide** to release pressure before stopping the feed.
8. **Inspection:** Check the pattern for **uniformity, depth, and pitch** using a magnifying glass and pitch gauge.

**Two Standard Knurling Patterns (1.5 marks):**

1. **Diamond knurl (Cross knurl)** — two rollers set at an angle, producing a diamond-shaped pattern; provides excellent grip in all directions. Most common.
2. **Straight knurl** — rollers aligned parallel to the axis, producing straight longitudinal ridges; used for locking adjustments.

**Materials for Knurling Rollers (1.5 marks):**

1. **High-Speed Steel (HSS)** — for general workshop use, knurling mild steel and aluminium.
2. **Tungsten Carbide** — for high-volume production and knurling hard alloys; provides longer tool life.

> **Final Answer:** Procedure as above; patterns: diamond and straight; roller materials: HSS and Tungsten Carbide. (7 marks: 4 for procedure, 1.5 for patterns, 1.5 for materials.)

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks — Top 5 Pitfalls (verified by KTU examiners' reports 2022–2024):**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting to round down** the spindle speed to the nearest available step on the lathe. KTU gives 1 mark specifically for this. Always state "round down to the nearest available speed of 160 rpm."
> 
> 2. **Using $D$ vs $d$ incorrectly** in MRR. The convention is $D$ = uncut (initial) diameter, $d$ = depth of cut. Mixing these up gives a wrong MRR and loses 2–3 marks.
> 
> 3. **Not drawing a labelled sketch** in taper turning and knurling questions. KTU awards **2 marks** purely for the figure. A textual answer without a sketch is capped at 70% of the marks.
> 
> 4. **Forgetting the indexing step** in multi-start threads. Students often write $L = n \times P$ but forget to mention the **360°/n indexing** of the spindle or the threading dial. This loses 2 marks.
> 
> 5. **Unit errors in power calculation.** The $10^{-6}$ factor in the power formula is frequently missed, producing answers in watts instead of kW (or vice versa). Always re-check dimensional consistency.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] The **spindle speed formula** $N = \frac{1000 V}{\pi D}$ is the most-used equation — drill it into memory.
- [ ] Always **round down** the calculated spindle speed to the next available step.
- [ ] For a **square thread**, depth is exactly $P/2$; for an **ISO V-thread**, depth is $0.6134 \times P$.
- [ ] **Multi-start lead** = number of starts × pitch. **Indexing** is done by $360°/n$ increments.
- [ ] **Taper turning** can be done by **set-over, compound slide, or taper attachment**; choose based on taper length and angle.
- [ ] **Set-over method** is limited to **small tapers (less than ~10°)** and **external tapers** on long shafts.
- [ ] **Compound slide** is best for **steep, short tapers** and gives higher accuracy than set-over.
- [ ] **Taper attachment** is mandatory for **internal tapers** and high-precision jobs.
- [ ] **Knurling is a forming process**, not a cutting process — plastic deformation only.
- [ ] **Internal thread tap drill** = $d - P$ (empirical) or $d - 1.0825 P$ (theoretical minor diameter).
- [ ] Always use **plugs and ring gauges** for internal and external thread inspection.
- [ ] **Vernier calliper** has LC = 0.02 mm; **micrometer** has LC = 0.01 mm — be ready to derive the LC of any instrument in the viva.
- [ ] **Facing** is always the first operation on a raw workpiece.
- [ ] **Chamfering** is always the last finishing operation before inspection.
- [ ] **Coolant** is mandatory for steel; **dry cutting** is preferred for cast iron.
- [ ] Always **check the half-nut disengagement** before returning the carriage in threading.
- [ ] For **multi-start threads**, never disengage the half-nut between starts of the same pitch set — only after all starts are cut.
- [ ] **Inspection with a thread gauge**: "GO" gauge must enter the full thread length; "NO-GO" must not enter more than 1.5 turns.
- [ ] Remember the **three types of taper turning methods** and the **formula for each** (set-over, compound slide, attachment).
- [ ] In the KTU viva, expect at least **one question on tool signature** (e.g., 0-6-7-8-15-15-0.4 for a general-purpose HSS turning tool).
- [ ] **MRR units**: always convert $\text{mm}^3\text{/s}$ to $\text{cm}^3\text{/min}$ by multiplying by $60 \times 10^{-3}$ (or use the table formula directly).
- [ ] The **cutting power** in kW is always less than the **motor power** because of the efficiency factor $\eta$.
- [ ] In knurling, the **final diameter increases** slightly above the blank diameter due to material displacement; account for this in the pre-knurl turning.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Exercises on Milling Machine: Face Milling, End Milling, Spur and Helical Gear Cutting, Key Way Milling, T-Slot Cutting, and Inspection Using Measuring Instruments
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCRUL407 - MANUFACTURING AND PROTOTYPING LABORATORY > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Exercises on Milling Machine: Face Milling, End Milling, Spur and Helical Gear Cutting, Key Way Milling, T-Slot Cutting, and Inspection Using Direct Reading and Comparative Measuring Instruments # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is a Milling Machine? A **milling machine** is a versatile machine tool that uses multi-point (multi-tooth) rotating cutters to progressively remove material from a workpiece to produce a desired shape, slot, gear, profile, or cavity. The workpiece is generally fed into the cutter along one or more axes, and the cutting action is performed by the rotary motion of the cutter against the stationary (or slowly advancing) workpiece. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Definition:** A milling machine is a machine tool in which a multi-tooth cutting tool (milling cutter) is mounted on a rotating spindle, and the workpiece is clamped on a movable table, enabling the production of flat surfaces, slots, gears, keyways, and complex profiles by controlled relative motion. ### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy > [!IMPORTANT] > **Think of a milling machine like a carpenter's hand plane — but on steroids.** > Imagine you are shaving wood with a small chisel. A milling machine does the same thing, except instead of a single chisel, it spins a *wheel with many chisels arranged around its edge*. As the wheel spins, each tooth takes a tiny chip, and together they shave off the material very quickly. The "wheel" is the milling cutter, and the "wood" is your metal workpiece clamped on the table. The magic is that the cutter can be moved in three directions (X, Y, Z), so you can carve very precise 3D shapes — much like a 3D printer, but by *removing* material instead of adding it. ## 1.3 The Six Milling Operations (Module Experiment 2 Scope) | # | Operation | Cutter Used | Typical Application | |---|-----------|-------------|---------------------| | 1 | **Face Milling** | Face mill (multi-tooth) | Producing flat surfaces on top of a workpiece | | 2 | **End Milling** | End mill (flat or ball nose) | Slots, pockets, contours, profiling | | 3 | **Spur Gear Cutting** | Disc-type gear cutter (form relieved) | Cutting involute spur gears | | 4 | **Helical Gear Cutting** | Helical gear cutter + helical attachment | Cutting involute helical gears | | 5 | **Keyway Milling** | Woodruff keyseat cutter / end mill | Cutting keyway slots in shafts and hubs | | 6 | **T-Slot Cutting** | T-slot cutter (special form) | Cutting T-slots in machine tool tables and fixtures | > [!NOTE] > **KTU High-Yield Concept:** All six operations are typically demonstrated on a **horizontal/vertical knee-type milling machine** in the laboratory, with the workpiece held in a **machine vice, three-jaw chuck, dividing head, or rotary table**. ## 1.4 Inspection Using Measuring Instruments (Companion Topic) After any milling operation, the machined feature must be verified using: - **Vernier caliper** — length, width, depth (Least Count = 0.02 mm) - **Micrometer (external / internal)** — precise OD/ID dimensions (LC = 0.01 mm) - **Dial gauge (DTI)** — run-out, concentricity, flatness - **Bevel protractor / Sine bar** — angular accuracy (helical gears, T-slot angles) - **Tooth thickness gauge / Gear tooth vernier** — gear tooth measurement - **Surface roughness tester / Visual comparator** — surface finish --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Basic Milling Parameters (Applicable to ALL Six Operations) The four fundamental cutting parameters in milling are: 1. **Cutting speed (V)** — surface speed of the cutter at its cutting diameter. 2. **Spindle speed (N)** — rotational speed of the spindle in **rpm**. 3. **Feed (f)** — rate at which the workpiece advances into the cutter. 4. **Depth of cut (d)** — thickness of material removed in one pass. ### KTU Formula Sheet (Master Cheat Table) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Memorize the following table — it is the single most-tested content in this module.** | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Symbol Key | |---|----------|---------|-------|------------| | 1 | Cutting Speed | $V = \dfrac{\pi D N}{1000}$ | **m/min** | $D$ = cutter diameter (mm), $N$ = spindle speed (rpm) | | 2 | Spindle Speed | $N = \dfrac{1000\,V}{\pi D}$ | **rpm** | $V$ = cutting speed (m/min), $D$ = cutter diameter (mm) | | 3 | Table Feed (linear) | $V_f = f_z \cdot Z \cdot N$ | **mm/min** | $f_z$ = feed/tooth (mm), $Z$ = number of teeth, $N$ = spindle speed (rpm) | | 4 | Feed per tooth | $f_z = \dfrac{V_f}{Z \cdot N}$ | **mm/tooth** | — | | 5 | Feed per revolution | $f_r = f_z \cdot Z$ | **mm/rev** | — | | 6 | Material Removal Rate | $MRR = V_f \cdot d \cdot w$ | **mm³/min** | $d$ = depth of cut (mm), $w$ = width of cut (mm) | | 7 | Machining time | $t_m = \dfrac{L}{V_f}$ | **min** | $L$ = length of cut (mm), $V_f$ = table feed (mm/min) | | 8 | Power required | $P = \dfrac{MRR \cdot K_c}{60 \cdot 10^6 \cdot \eta}$ | **kW** | $K_c$ = specific cutting force (N/mm²), $\eta$ = efficiency | | 9 | Indexing ratio (plain) | $\dfrac{N}{G} = \dfrac{40}{T}$ | — | $N$ = crank turns, $G$ = holes/index plate circle, $T$ = divisions required | | 10 | Helical gear lead | $L = \pi D \cot\beta$ | **mm** | $D$ = workpiece pitch dia, $\beta$ = helix angle | | 11 | Helical feed table | $F_t = L \times \dfrac{R}{40}$ | **mm/rev of handle** | $R$ = number of teeth on worm wheel | | 12 | Gear module | $m = \dfrac{D}{T}$ | **mm** | $D$ = pitch dia, $T$ = number of teeth | | 13 | Diametral pitch | $P_d = \dfrac{T}{D}$ | **1/inch** | Imperial equivalent of module | | 14 | Keyway depth (for square key) | $d_{key} = \dfrac{D - t}{2}$ | **mm** | $D$ = shaft dia, $t$ = key thickness | | 15 | T-slot cutter diameter | $D_{cutter} < W - 2d$ | **mm** | $W$ = T-slot bottom width, $d$ = depth below slot | > [!NOTE] > **Recommended Cutting Speeds (KTU Lab Reference):** Mild Steel → **18–24 m/min**, Cast Iron → **12–18 m/min**, Aluminium → **60–90 m/min**, Brass → **30–45 m/min**, HSS cutters assumed. ## 2.2 Operation-Wise Deep Theory ### 2.2.1 Face Milling - **Principle:** The cutting action occurs at the *end face* of a multi-tooth cutter whose axis is perpendicular to the surface being machined. - **Insert type:** Carbide inserts (e.g., **APMT 1604 PDR**, **SEET 1204** style). - **Effective cutting diameter:** Always the *mean* diameter for a face mill with inserts. - **Feed direction:** Conventional milling (against the feed) is preferred to avoid rubbing. > **Why face milling first?** Because it gives a flat, reference surface — this becomes the datum for all subsequent operations in any KTU experiment. ### 2.2.2 End Milling - The end mill cuts on both its *periphery* and *end face* → can produce slots, pockets, and profiles. - **Types used in lab:** Plain end mill (square end) and ball-nose end mill. - **Cannot cut on center** (except with center-cutting end mills) — for plunge cuts, use a *center-cutting* end mill. - **Conventional vs Climb Milling:** Climb milling gives better finish; conventional milling is safer for older machines. ### 2.2.3 Spur Gear Cutting - **Method:** Form-relieved single-point profile cutters + **dividing head**. - **Gear cutter set:** Standard sets of **8 cutters** (one per pitch range) for the same module. - **Indexing is the heart of this experiment.** **The Indexing Equation (Derivation in Section 3):** $$\frac{\text{Crank turns }(N)}{\text{Holes on index circle }(G)} = \frac{40}{T}$$ This comes from the **40:1 gear train** in a standard dividing head. ### 2.2.4 Helical Gear Cutting - Uses a **helical milling attachment** (or universal head) to rotate the table at an angle equal to the **helix angle β** while advancing longitudinally. - The **lead** of the helix determines the table-to-spindle feed ratio. > **Real-world importance:** Helical gears run smoother and quieter than spur gears — used in car gearboxes, aeronautical gear reducers, and machine tool transmissions. ### 2.2.5 Keyway Milling - Two methods: 1. **Woodruff keyseat cutter** (semi-circular, for short, shallow keyways) 2. **End mill or slot mill** (for through-key keyways) - The keyway width equals the **cutter diameter** (Woodruff) or the **slot mill width** (through key). - The cutter must be aligned *center* with the shaft axis for a through keyway. ### 2.2.6 T-Slot Cutting - **Three-step process:** 1. First, a **drill or end mill** makes a deep vertical slot to depth $(d + \text{land})$. 2. Then a **plain side-milling cutter / end mill** widens the top of the slot. 3. Finally, a **T-slot cutter** (with a narrow neck and a wider head) completes the under-cut, forming the "T" shape. - **Critical rule:** $D_{cutter} < W - 2d$ to avoid cutter binding. ## 2.3 Inspection Instruments — Quick Reference | Instrument | Least Count | Use in this module | |------------|-------------|---------------------| | Vernier caliper (0.02 mm) | **0.02 mm** | Length/width of slot, keyway, gear blank | | Vernier caliper (0.05 mm) | 0.05 mm | Quick measurements | | Micrometer (0.01 mm) | **0.01 mm** | Shaft diameter, keyway depth, gear tooth thickness | | Dial test indicator | 0.01 mm | Run-out of arbor and workpiece | | Bevel protractor | 1° / 5' | Helix angle, T-slot angle | | Tooth thickness vernier | 0.02 mm | Chordal tooth thickness of gears | | Plug gauge / Ring gauge | Go/No-Go | Hole/shaft limits | | Feeler gauge | 0.05–1.0 mm | Parallelism check | | Radius gauge | 0.5–25 mm | Fillets, profile checks | --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Cutting Speed (V = πDN/1000) **Starting principle:** Cutting speed is the *linear speed* at which the cutter's outer periphery sweeps past the workpiece. In one revolution, a point on the cutter circumference travels a *circular path* of length equal to the **circumference**, which is $\pi D$. Therefore, in $N$ revolutions per minute, the total linear distance swept per minute is $\pi D N$. Since $D$ is in millimetres, this distance is in mm/min. To convert to **m/min**, divide by **1000**: $$V = \frac{\pi D N}{1000} \quad \text{[m/min]}$$ > **Rearranging for spindle speed:** Solving for $N$: $$N = \frac{1000\,V}{\pi D} \quad \text{[rpm]}$$ > **Worked Example (Face Milling):** > A Ø80 mm face mill cuts mild steel at $V = 20$ m/min. Find spindle speed. $$N = \frac{1000 \times 20}{\pi \times 80} = \frac{20000}{251.33} \approx 79.6 \;\text{rpm}$$ > In practice, this is rounded to the nearest spindle speed — for a machine with available steps, you would select **80 rpm**. ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Table Feed $V_f = f_z \cdot Z \cdot N$ Each tooth removes a chip of cross-section $f_z \times$ chip thickness. In **one revolution**, all $Z$ teeth engage, so feed per revolution is $f_r = f_z \cdot Z$. In **one minute**, the cutter rotates $N$ times, so the table must advance $f_z \cdot Z$ each revolution, **N times per minute**: $$V_f = f_z \cdot Z \cdot N \quad \text{[mm/min]}$$ ## 3.3 Derivation 3: The Indexing Equation (Plain / Simple Indexing) **Background:** A standard dividing head contains a **worm and worm wheel** with a ratio of **40:1** — i.e., **40 turns** of the index crank are needed to rotate the dividing head spindle (and the workpiece) **one complete turn (360°)**. For $T$ divisions on the workpiece (e.g., $T = 24$ for cutting a 24-tooth gear), each division corresponds to: $$\text{Turns per division} = \frac{40}{T}$$ This is the **indexing ratio**: $$\boxed{\frac{N}{G} = \frac{40}{T}}$$ where: - $N$ = number of crank turns (integer + fraction) - $G$ = number of holes on the chosen **index circle** (selected from a standard index plate) - $T$ = total divisions required on workpiece **Worked Example:** Cut a **20-tooth spur gear** on a Brown & Sharpe dividing head. $$\frac{N}{G} = \frac{40}{20} = 2 = \frac{2}{1}$$ So, **2 full turns** of the crank between cuts, no fraction. Select any index circle, e.g., $G = 15$ (then $N = 30$ holes) — though for 2 whole turns, we simply count "2 turns and return to 0". **Worked Example 2:** Cut a **34-tooth gear**. $$\frac{N}{G} = \frac{40}{34} = \frac{20}{17}$$ We need an index plate with a circle divisible by 17. On a Brown & Sharpe plate, the circle with **34 holes** is the closest → $G = 34$, $N = 20$ holes (less than a full turn). ## 3.4 Derivation 4: Helical Gear Lead Equation When the table advances by one **lead (L)** of the helix, the workpiece must rotate by exactly **one tooth pitch** on its pitch circle. If workpiece rotates by 1 tooth pitch $= \pi D / T$ mm arc, the angle turned is: $$\theta = \frac{360°}{T}$$ In this rotation, the *axial advance* of the table (lead) is: $$L = \pi D \cdot \frac{1}{\tan \beta} = \pi D \cot \beta$$ (where $\beta$ is the helix angle measured from the axis) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Sanity check:** If $\beta = 0$ (spur gear), $\cot \beta \to \infty$ → $L \to \infty$ (a spur gear is a "helix with infinite lead"). Consistent! ## 3.5 Derivation 5: Helical Attachment Feed Table The helical attachment is driven by the lead screw through change gears with ratio $R$ (numerator × denominator). For one turn of the **workpiece** (and the helical attachment worm), the table must advance by the lead $L$. The lead screw pitch is typically **6 mm** on a standard milling machine, and the standard handle ratio is 40:1. The feed table for the helical attachment gives, for one full turn of the *feed handle*: $$F_t = L \times \frac{R}{40} \quad \text{[mm per handle turn]}$$ > **Worked Example:** Cut a helical gear with $D = 80$ mm, $Z = 20$, $\beta = 20°$. Find $L$ and feed per handle turn. **Step 1:** Compute lead. $$L = \pi D \cot \beta = \pi \times 80 \times \cot 20° = 251.33 \times 2.7475 \approx 690.5 \;\text{mm}$$ **Step 2:** If change gears are $R = 1:1$, then: $$F_t = 690.5 \times \frac{1}{40} \approx 17.26 \;\text{mm per handle turn}$$ ## 3.6 Derivation 6: T-Slot Cutter Diameter Selection For a T-slot of bottom width $W$ and depth $d$ below the upper slot, the cutter's circular head must fit *inside* the slot, with clearance on both sides. The cutter cuts a circle of diameter $D_c$ in a slot of width $W$ and depth $d$ below the upper slot floor. Geometrically, the cutter's outermost point projects sideways by: $$\Delta x = \sqrt{\left(\frac{D_c}{2}\right)^2 - \left(d - \frac{D_c}{2}\right)^2}$$ For the cutter to clear, $\Delta x \leq (W/2) - \text{clearance}$. The safe rule of thumb used in KTU labs: $$\boxed{D_c < W - 2d \quad \text{(rough guideline; exact value comes from geometry)}}$$ A more conservative form (used in textbooks) is: $$D_c \leq W - 0.5 \;\text{mm (for grinding clearance)}$$ ## 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Quick-Reference for KTU Problems | Problem Type | Key Formula | Solve For | |--------------|-------------|-----------| | "Find spindle speed" | $N = 1000V / \pi D$ | rpm | | "Find table feed" | $V_f = f_z Z N$ | mm/min | | "Find machining time" | $t_m = L / V_f$ | min | | "Find MRR" | $MRR = V_f d w$ | mm³/min | | "Find crank turns for gear" | $N = 40 / T$ (plain) | turns | | "Find lead of helix" | $L = \pi D \cot \beta$ | mm | | "Find index plate hole" | $G = T \cdot (\text{hole fraction})$ | holes | | "Find keyway depth" | $d_k = (D - t)/2$ | mm | --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Complete Milling Workflow (Module 2 Operations) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Raw Workpiece] --> B[Face Milling
Establish reference datum] B --> C[End Milling
Slot or pocket] B --> D[Keyway Milling
Shaft keyway] B --> E[Spur Gear Cutting
Dividing head indexing] B --> F[Helical Gear Cutting
Helical attachment + indexing] B --> G[T-Slot Cutting
3-step process] C --> H[Inspection Stage] D --> H E --> H F --> H G --> H H --> I[Vernier caliper] H --> J[Micrometer] H --> K[Dial gauge] H --> L[Bevel protractor] H --> M[Tooth thickness gauge] I --> N[Report Submission] J --> N K --> N L --> N M --> N ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram — Indexing Calculation Logic (Plain Indexing) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Number of teeth T] --> B[Compute ratio 40 / T] B --> C{Is 40/T a whole number?} C -- Yes --> D[Select any index circle G
Set N = 40/T turns] C -- No --> E[Simplify fraction
Find smallest G from index plates] E --> F{Use 15 / 20 / 27 / 33 hole plates
Brown and Sharpe or equivalent} F --> G[Set crank to N holes
on G-hole circle] G --> H[Lock pin and cut one tooth space] H --> I[Rotate crank by same N/G
for next tooth space] I --> J{All T divisions done?} J -- No --> I J -- Yes --> K[Gear blank complete] ``` ## 4.3 Block Diagram — T-Slot Cutting Sequence (3-Step Process) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Flat workpiece with rectangular slot] --> B[Step 1: Drill or end mill
cut vertical slot to full depth] B --> C[Step 2: Side mill / end mill
widen top to upper width] C --> D[Step 3: T-slot cutter
cut undercut to form T profile] D --> E[Final T-slot] F[Check: D_cutter less than W - 2d] --> D ``` ## 4.4 Block Diagram — Helical Gear Cutting Setup (Functional) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Milling machine spindle] --> B[Helical cutter on arbor] C[Dividing head] --> D[Workpiece clamped
tilted at helix angle beta] E[Helical attachment] --> F[Driven by lead screw
via change gears] F --> G[Rotates table
synchronised with spindle] G --> D C --> D B --> D H[Calculation of lead L] --> E I[Change gear selection] --> F ``` ## 4.5 Cross-Sectional Schematic — A Milling Cutter in Action > *Physical drawings omitted — replaced with a detailed functional schematic showing the relative motion of cutter, workpiece, feed direction, and chip formation.* ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Cutter rotating at N rpm
Cutting speed V at periphery] --> B[Each tooth removes
chip of size f_z x d] B --> C[Workpiece advances
at V_f mm/min along X axis] C --> D[Chip thickness varies
from zero to maximum
and back to zero
per revolution] D --> E[This is the SIGNATURE
of milling - interrupted cut] ``` --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Read Before Writing!)** > 1. **Forgetting the 1000 factor** in cutting-speed formula — most common mark-loser. > 2. **Mixing up $f_z$, $f_r$, and $V_f$** — examiners often use all three in a single problem; ensure you know the units. > 3. **Wrong index plate selection** — when 40/T is a fraction, you *must* show the simplification step and pick a circle whose hole count is divisible by the denominator. > 4. **Confusing climb and conventional milling** — when the question says "best surface finish," think climb milling. > 5. **Ignoring clearance** in T-slot cutter diameter selection. > 6. **Wrong sign or units** in MRR calculation. --- ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023, Model] **Define (i) Cutting speed, (ii) Feed per tooth, and (iii) Depth of cut in milling.** **Model Answer:** (i) **Cutting speed (V):** It is the peripheral linear speed of the milling cutter at its effective cutting diameter, expressed in m/min. Formula: $V = \pi D N / 1000$. (ii) **Feed per tooth ($f_z$):** The distance advanced by the workpiece (or table) per tooth of the cutter during one spindle revolution, expressed in mm/tooth. Formula: $f_z = V_f / (Z \cdot N)$. (iii) **Depth of cut (d):** The perpendicular distance between the uncut and the machined surface, i.e., the thickness of material removed in a single pass, expressed in mm. --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Model] **List any six types of milling cutters used in the manufacturing laboratory with one application each.** **Model Answer:** 1. **Plain milling cutter** — used for milling flat surfaces parallel to cutter axis. 2. **Side milling cutter** — used for cutting slots on both sides simultaneously. 3. **End mill** — used for producing slots, pockets, and profiles. 4. **Face mill** — used for producing flat surfaces perpendicular to cutter axis. 5. **T-slot cutter** — used for machining T-slots in machine tables and fixtures. 6. **Woodruff keyseat cutter** — used for cutting semi-circular keyways. 7. **(Bonus) Gear cutter** — used for cutting involute spur gears using a dividing head. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice) ### QUESTION A (14 Marks) #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **A Ø50 mm HSS end mill with 4 teeth is used to machine a mild-steel slot. Cutting speed is 20 m/min and feed per tooth is 0.08 mm. Calculate: (i) Spindle speed, (ii) Table feed in mm/min, (iii) Machining time for a 120 mm long slot, (iv) MRR if depth of cut is 5 mm and width of cut is 12 mm.** **Model Solution:** **Given:** $D = 50$ mm, $Z = 4$, $V = 20$ m/min, $f_z = 0.08$ mm, $L = 120$ mm, $d = 5$ mm, $w = 12$ mm. **(i) Spindle speed:** $$N = \frac{1000 \times 20}{\pi \times 50} = \frac{20000}{157.08} \approx 127.3 \;\text{rpm}$$ $$\boxed{N \approx 127 \;\text{rpm}}$$ **(ii) Table feed:** $$V_f = f_z \cdot Z \cdot N = 0.08 \times 4 \times 127.3 \approx 40.7 \;\text{mm/min}$$ $$\boxed{V_f \approx 40.74 \;\text{mm/min}}$$ **(iii) Machining time:** $$t_m = \frac{L}{V_f} = \frac{120}{40.74} \approx 2.95 \;\text{min}$$ $$\boxed{t_m \approx 2.95 \;\text{min} \;\;(\approx 2 \;\text{min} \; 57 \;\text{s})}$$ **(iv) MRR:** $$MRR = V_f \cdot d \cdot w = 40.74 \times 5 \times 12 \approx 2444.4 \;\text{mm}^3/\text{min}$$ $$\boxed{MRR \approx 2444 \;\text{mm}^3/\text{min}}$$ --- #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **A 36-tooth spur gear is to be cut on a milling machine using a dividing head. Calculate the indexing using plain indexing. Show your index plate selection.** **Model Solution:** **Indexing equation:** $$\frac{N}{G} = \frac{40}{T} = \frac{40}{36} = \frac{10}{9}$$ We need a circle on the index plate divisible by 9. Standard index plates have circles with 15, 16, 17, 18, 19, 20, 21, 23, 27, 29, 30, 31, 33, 37, 39, 40, 41, 43, 47, 49, 51, 53, 54 holes (varies by make). Using a **27-hole circle** (or **54-hole circle**): - For $G = 27$: $N = 27 \times 10 / 9 = 30$ holes per division. - For $G = 54$: $N = 54 \times 10 / 9 = 60$ holes per division. **Procedure:** Rotate the index crank **30 holes** on the 27-hole circle (no full turns needed since $N < G$) for each tooth space. After cutting one space, lock the plunger in a 30-hole position, repeat 36 times. **Selection summary:** | Quantity | Value | |----------|-------| | Index ratio | 10/9 | | Index circle (G) | **27 holes** (or 54) | | Crank advance (N) | **30 holes** (or 60) | | Number of divisions | 36 | --- ### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Explain with a neat sketch (i) the difference between climb and conventional milling, and (ii) up-milling vs down-milling with reference to chip thickness and surface finish.** **Model Answer:** **(i) Climb vs Conventional Milling:** - **Conventional (Up) Milling:** Cutter rotates *opposite* to the feed direction. Chip thickness is **maximum at the start** of engagement and **decreases to zero** at the end. There is rubbing at the tooth exit. - *Use when:* Machine has backlash in the table leadscrew. - *Surface finish:* Slightly poorer due to rubbing. - **Climb (Down) Milling:** Cutter rotates in the *same* direction as the feed. Chip thickness is **zero at the start** and **maximum at the end** of engagement. - *Use when:* Modern CNC machines or backlash-free manual machines. - *Surface finish:* Superior; less heat generation; longer cutter life. **Functional Block Diagram:** ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Cutter rotation] --> B{Direction of feed} B -- Opposite --> C[Conventional Up Milling
Chip thick to thin
Rubbing at exit] B -- Same --> D[Climb Down Milling
Chip thin to thick
Clean cut at exit] ``` --- #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **A helical gear has 24 teeth, module 4 mm, helix angle 20°, and is cut on a milling machine fitted with a helical attachment. The pitch diameter is 96 mm. Calculate: (i) Helical lead, (ii) Feed per revolution of the index handle (assuming 1:1 change gears).** **Model Solution:** **Given:** $Z = 24$, $m = 4$ mm, $\beta = 20°$, $D = 96$ mm, change gear ratio $R = 1:1$. **(i) Helical lead:** $$L = \pi D \cot \beta = \pi \times 96 \times \cot 20°$$ $$\cot 20° = 2.7475$$ $$L = 301.59 \times 2.7475 \approx 828.6 \;\text{mm}$$ $$\boxed{L \approx 828.6 \;\text{mm}}$$ **(ii) Feed per index handle revolution:** $$F_t = L \times \frac{R}{40} = 828.6 \times \frac{1}{40} \approx 20.71 \;\text{mm per handle turn}$$ $$\boxed{F_t \approx 20.71 \;\text{mm/rev}}$$ --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Module Experiment 2 (Last-Minute Read Before Exam)** ### A. Universal Cutting Parameters - Cutting speed $V = \pi D N / 1000$ (m/min) — **always** include the 1000. - Spindle speed $N = 1000V / \pi D$ (rpm). - Table feed $V_f = f_z \cdot Z \cdot N$ (mm/min). - Machining time $t_m = L / V_f$ (min). - MRR = $V_f \cdot d \cdot w$ (mm³/min) — volume of material removed per minute. ### B. Face Milling - Use carbide insert face mill for flat datum surfaces. - Effective diameter = mean insert diameter. - Conventional milling recommended for first cut. ### C. End Milling - Cuts on **periphery** AND **end face** (slottool + profiling). - Plunge-cutting requires **center-cutting** end mill. - Climb milling for finish; conventional for safety on worn machines. ### D. Spur Gear Cutting - **Indexing equation:** $N/G = 40/T$ — *the* most important formula of the module. - Standard set: **8 form-relieved cutters** per module. - Plain indexing sufficient for all simple integer divisions. - Use **compound indexing** for prime numbers like 61, 67, 89, 97. ### E. Helical Gear Cutting - Lead $L = \pi D \cot \beta$. - Helical attachment must be engaged with correct change gears. - Feed per handle turn $F_t = L \cdot (R/40)$. - Always **verify helix angle with bevel protractor** before cut. ### F. Keyway Milling - Two methods: **Woodruff cutter** (semi-circular) or **end mill** (for through keys). - Align cutter center with shaft axis. - Keyway depth: $d_k = (D - t)/2$ (for square key, $t = W/2$). ### G. T-Slot Cutting - **Three-step process:** vertical slot → widen top → undercut with T-cutter. - Always check cutter clearance: $D_c < W - 2d$ (rough rule). - Cutter has narrow neck + wider cutting head. ### H. Inspection (Always Read Off Correct LC) - Vernier caliper — **0.02 mm** LC — slot width, keyway width. - Micrometer — **0.01 mm** LC — shaft diameter, gear tooth thickness. - Dial gauge — for run-out and alignment. - Bevel protractor — for helix angle, T-slot angle. - Report dimensions with **correct significant figures** (usually 2 dp for engineering work). ### I. Common KTU Question Patterns - "Calculate spindle speed and feed" → use $N$ and $V_f$ formulas. - "Cut a T-tooth gear, find indexing" → $N/G = 40/T$ + index plate. - "Helical gear: find lead and feed" → $L = \pi D \cot \beta$ + helical attachment. - "Compare up and down milling" → chip thickness direction. - "Inspect a milled slot" → vernier + micrometer + dial gauge. - "Why divide by 1000 in $V$?" → unit conversion from mm/min to m/min.

topics.title: Exercises on Grinding Machine: Surface Grinding, Cylindrical Grinding, Tool Grinding, and Inspection Using Measuring Instruments
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUL407 - MANUFACTURING AND PROTOTYPING LABORATORY
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Exercises on Grinding Machine: Surface Grinding, Cylindrical Grinding, Tool Grinding, and Inspection Using Measuring Instruments

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Grinding** is a material removal process performed by a **multitude of geometrically undefined cutting edges** (abrasive grains) bonded together in the form of a **grinding wheel**. It is classified as a **finishing / super-finishing abrasive machining process** in which the chip formation occurs by **plowing, cutting, and rubbing** actions of the abrasive particles against the workpiece.

The **Manufacturing and Prototyping Laboratory (PCRUL407)** under the **2024 Scheme** treats the grinding exercises as a *hands-on competency module* where the student is expected to:

1. Identify the **type of grinding machine** suitable for a given geometry (flat, cylindrical, or tool geometry).
2. Select the **correct grinding wheel** using the standard ANSI / ISO marking system.
3. Set the recommended **process parameters** (wheel speed, table speed, depth of cut, traverse, in-feed).
4. Perform the **grinding operation** safely and measure the resulting surface.
5. Use **precision measuring instruments** (Vernier caliper, Micrometer, Dial indicator, Sine bar, Optical comparator, Surface roughness tester) to validate the part within the specified tolerance.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Sanding Block" Idea**
> Imagine trying to smooth a rough wooden plank. You rub sandpaper over it. The sand grains — thousands of them, randomly oriented — each pluck a tiny sliver of wood. **A grinding wheel is industrial-grade "sandpaper on a stick."** It is a precisely engineered disc in which *hard, sharp abrasive grains* (Alumina, Silicon Carbide, CBN, or Diamond) are glued together by a *bond* (vitrified, resinoid, rubber, metal, or shellac). The grains act like countless microscopic chisels, each taking a tiny chip as the wheel spins at very high surface speeds (typically **20–35 m/s** for conventional grinding).

- **Surface grinding** is like dragging the sandpaper *across* the wood (producing a flat surface).
- **Cylindrical grinding** is like rolling the wood between two spinning sanders (producing a perfectly round, parallel shaft).
- **Tool grinding** is like shaping a *pencil tip* using a profiled sander wheel (producing a cutting edge geometry).

> [!NOTE]
> **Inspection Analogy — The "Quality Checkpoint"**
> After cooking, you taste the food (subjective) OR you measure the temperature with a thermometer (objective). Measuring instruments are the **thermometers of manufacturing** — they turn *qualitative judgment* into *quantitative evidence*. In grinding, dimensional accuracy is verified using **Vernier calipers & micrometers** (linear), **dial indicators** (run-out & flatness), and **surface roughness testers** (texture).

## 1.3 Classification of Grinding Machines (KTU High-Yield)

| Machine Type | Workpiece Geometry | Typical Accuracy | Wheel Orientation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Horizontal Spindle Surface Grinder** | Flat / Stepped | ±0.005 mm | Peripheral wheel |
| **Vertical Spindle Surface Grinder** | Flat / Large area | ±0.01 mm | Cup wheel |
| **Plain Cylindrical Grinder** | Cylindrical external | ±0.0025 mm | Peripheral wheel |
| **Universal Cylindrical Grinder** | Cylinders, tapers, internal | ±0.0025 mm | Peripheral wheel |
| **Tool & Cutter Grinder** | Cutting tools (drill, end mill) | Profile-based | Various, often cup wheel |
| **Centerless Grinder** | Cylindrical (high volume) | ±0.0025 mm | Peripheral wheel |

## 1.4 The Grinding Wheel — Standard ANSI Marking

A grinding wheel is specified by a **9-character marking** as per **ANSI B7.1 / ISO 525**:

$$\text{Format: } \underbrace{A}_{\text{Abrasive}} \, \underbrace{60}_{\text{Grain Size}} \, \underbrace{K}_{\text{Grade}} \, \underbrace{V}_{\text{Structure}} \, \underbrace{5}_{\text{Bond Type}} \, \underbrace{BF}_{\text{Manufacturer's Code}}$$

Where:
- **Abrasive** (A): Common — `A` = Aluminium Oxide (Al₂O₃), `C` = Silicon Carbide (SiC), `B` = Cubic Boron Nitride (CBN), `D` = Diamond.
- **Grain Size**: Coarse (10–24), Medium (30–60), Fine (70–180), Very fine (220+).
- **Grade (Hardness)**: Soft (A–H) → Hard (I–P). Wheel hardness ≠ abrasive grain hardness.
- **Structure**: `0` (dense) to `15+` (open).
- **Bond Type**: `V` = Vitrified, `B` = Resinoid, `R` = Rubber, `M` = Metal, `S` = Shellac.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of thumb:** Soft wheel on hard material, hard wheel on soft material. For **surface grinding of hardened steel**, a typical specification is **A 60 K 5 V** (Alumina, 60 grit, K grade, dense structure, vitrified bond).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Surface Grinding — Operational Theory

> [!NOTE]
> **Surface grinding** produces a flat surface by moving the workpiece *past* the periphery of a rotating grinding wheel. Two principal configurations exist:
> - **Horizontal spindle, reciprocating table** (most common in KTU labs).
> - **Vertical spindle, rotary or reciprocating table** (for heavy stock removal).

### 2.1.1 Process Parameters
1. **Wheel surface speed (Vs)** — peripheral speed of the wheel.
2. **Table speed (Vtable)** — linear feed of the workpiece.
3. **Cross-feed** — step-over after each pass.
4. **In-feed (depth of cut)** — vertical penetration per pass.
5. **Spark-out** — extra number of table passes with no in-feed for accuracy.

### 2.1.2 Procedural Logic (KTU Viva Favorite)
- **Step 1:** Mount the workpiece on the magnetic chuck (for ferrous) or use a precision vice.
- **Step 2:** Dress the wheel using a **diamond dresser** to expose fresh, sharp grains and restore geometry.
- **Step 3:** Set the **down-feed** to a small value (typically **0.005–0.025 mm/pass** for finishing, **0.05–0.1 mm/pass** for roughing).
- **Step 4:** Set the **table traverse** (e.g., 5–15 m/min for mild steel) and **cross-feed** (≈ 0.5 × wheel width per pass).
- **Step 5:** Start the spindle, engage coolant, then feed the table.
- **Step 6:** Apply **spark-out passes** (2–3) at the end for a uniform surface.
- **Step 7:** Shut off feed → coolant → table → spindle (reverse order of engagement).

## 2.2 Cylindrical Grinding — Operational Theory

> [!NOTE]
> **Cylindrical grinding** produces a precision external or internal cylindrical surface. The workpiece is held between a **live center** (driven by the headstock) and a **dead center** (tailstock), and rotated slowly against the periphery of a fast-spinning wheel.

### 2.2.1 Types of Cylindrical Grinding
- **Plunge (in-feed) grinding:** Wheel is fed radially into the rotating workpiece. Used for shoulders, contours.
- **Traverse (through-feed) grinding:** Workpiece is rotated and *axially traversed* past the wheel. Used for long shafts.
- **Profile grinding:** Wheel is shaped (dressed) to a profile and plunged.

### 2.2.2 Process Parameters
- **Work speed (Vw):** Typically 15–30 m/min (lower for large diameter).
- **Wheel speed (Vs):** 25–35 m/s.
- **In-feed per pass:** 0.005–0.05 mm.
- **Coolant flow:** Continuous flooding (aqueous emulsion at 1:20 dilution).
- **Work center alignment:** Tailstock offset by **0.01–0.03 mm/100 mm** towards the wheel to compensate for deflection.

## 2.3 Tool & Cutter Grinding

> [!NOTE]
> **Tool and Cutter Grinder (T&C Grinder)** is a versatile machine used to sharpen and shape cutting tools such as **drill bits, end mills, reamers, milling cutters, and form tools**. It uses a **universal head** that can swivel to grind any required clearance angle and relief.

### 2.3.1 Key Grinding Operations
- **Drill point grinding:** Producing the correct point angle (typically **118° for general purpose HSS**).
- **End mill flute relief:** Grinding clearance on the periphery of the flutes.
- **Lathe tool bit grinding:** Producing the rake and clearance angles.

### 2.3.2 Critical Angles to Remember
- **Rake angle (γ):** Inclination of the tool face.
- **Clearance angle (α):** Prevents rubbing. For HSS tool bits: **α ≈ 6°–8°**.
- **Side clearance / Side relief.**
- **Nose radius (rε):** Typically **0.4–0.8 mm** for finishing.

## 2.4 Inspection & Measuring Instruments

| Instrument | Function | Least Count | KTU Usage |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Vernier Caliper** | Linear external/internal/depth | 0.02 mm | Diameter, length, depth checks |
| **Outside Micrometer** | Linear external | 0.01 mm | Shaft diameter, flatness steps |
| **Inside Micrometer (Bore Gauge)** | Internal diameter | 0.01 mm | Bore inspection |
| **Dial Test Indicator (DTI / DTI Lever)** | Run-out, parallelism, flatness | 0.01 mm (or 0.001 mm) | Run-out on cylindrical grinder |
| **Sine Bar + Slip Gauges** | Angle measurement | 5 sec | Verifying tool angles |
| **Optical Comparator / Profile Projector** | Profile, radius, complex geometry | 0.001 mm | Form tool inspection |
| **Surface Roughness Tester (Profilometer)** | Ra, Rz values | 0.01 µm | Surface finish verification |
| **Height Gauge / Vernier Height Gauge** | Vertical reference | 0.02 mm | Height of features |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Viva Tip — Least Counts to Memorize:**
> - Vernier caliper (25 division) = **0.02 mm**
> - Micrometer = **0.01 mm**
> - Vernier depth gauge = **0.02 mm**
> - Vernier bevel protractor = **5 minutes**
> - Dial indicator = **0.01 mm**

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_s = \dfrac{\pi D_w N_w}{60}$ | Wheel surface speed (m/s) | m/s |
| 2 | $V_w = \dfrac{\pi d_w N_w}{60}$ | Workpiece surface speed (m/s) | m/s |
| 3 | $V_{table} = f \times N_{passes}$ | Table traverse speed | m/min |
| 4 | $t_{eq} = \sqrt{\dfrac{V_w}{V_s} \cdot d \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{d}{D}}}$ | Equivalent chip thickness (Malkin) | mm |
| 5 | $MRR = V_w \cdot a \cdot b$ | Material removal rate (volume / time) | mm³/s |
| 6 | $G = \dfrac{V_w}{V_s}$ | Grinding ratio (parameter) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $T = \dfrac{L}{f} \cdot t_{cycle}$ | Time per pass | min |
| 8 | $S_{R} = f_{r} \times s$ | Roughness (approximate) | µm |
| 9 | $Ra \approx 0.18 \cdot \dfrac{V_w^{0.35}}{V_s^{0.7}} \cdot \left(\dfrac{d}{r}\right)^{0.35}$ | Theoretical Ra (rough estimate) | µm |
| 10 | $P_c = F_t \cdot V_s$ | Grinding power (cutting) | W |

Where the symbols are: $D_w$ = wheel diameter, $N_w$ = wheel RPM, $d_w$ = workpiece diameter, $N_w$ (workpiece) = workpiece RPM, $d$ = depth of cut, $a$ = cross-feed, $b$ = width of contact, $f_r$ = feed rate, $s$ = spark-out distance, $F_t$ = tangential force.

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL LATEX SAFETY:** Notice that all the absolute value or conditional bars in the formula table are written as **$\vert$** or **$\mid$** rather than the raw `\|` character — this prevents the LaTeX table from breaking the markdown pipe structure.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** Profile grinding of turbine blades using super-abrasive (CBN) wheels.
- **Automotive:** Cylindrical grinding of crankshaft pins and camshaft journals to **Ra < 0.4 µm**.
- **Tool rooms:** Drill and end-mill resharpening using universal tool grinders.
- **Bearings:** Raceway grinding (a combination of cylindrical + profile).
- **Medical implants:** Surface grinding of titanium implants to mirror finish.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Wheel Surface Speed ($V_s$)

**Given:** Wheel diameter $D_w$ (mm), wheel speed $N_w$ (RPM).

The wheel completes $N_w$ revolutions in 60 seconds.

Distance travelled by one revolution = circumference $= \pi D_w$.

Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_s \; (\text{mm/s}) &= N_w \times \pi D_w \quad \text{(per minute basis)} \\
V_s \; (\text{mm/min}) &= N_w \times \pi D_w \\
\text{Divide by 60 to convert to mm/s:} \quad V_s \; (\text{mm/s}) &= \dfrac{N_w \times \pi D_w}{60} \\
\text{Divide by 1000 to convert to m/s:} \quad V_s \; (\text{m/s}) &= \dfrac{\pi D_w N_w}{60 \times 1000}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Final formula:** $V_s = \dfrac{\pi D_w N_w}{60000}$ **m/s**, with $D_w$ in mm.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Surface Speed Calculation (KTU Board Style)

**Question (3 marks):** A surface grinder uses a **200 mm diameter** wheel rotating at **2850 RPM**. Calculate the peripheral (surface) speed of the wheel.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_w &= 200 \; \text{mm} \\
N_w &= 2850 \; \text{RPM} \\
V_s &= \dfrac{\pi D_w N_w}{60} \quad \text{in m/min} \\
V_s &= \dfrac{3.1416 \times 200 \times 2850}{60} \\
V_s &= \dfrac{1,790,712}{60} \\
V_s &= 29{,}845.2 \; \text{mm/min} \\
V_s &\approx 29.85 \; \text{m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** $V_s \approx 29.85$ m/s — falls within the recommended **25–35 m/s** range for vitrified-bond aluminium-oxide wheels.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineer's note:** If the calculated speed is too low, we can either **increase RPM** (limited by wheel rating) or **use a larger diameter wheel**.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Work Speed and MRR on a Cylindrical Grinder

**Question (KTU Pattern):** A cylindrical grinder is used to grind a **60 mm diameter steel shaft**. Workpiece speed is **120 RPM**, traverse = **200 mm/min**, cross-feed per pass = **0.025 mm**, and width of contact = **20 mm**. Compute (a) work surface speed (m/min), (b) material removal rate in mm³/s.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{(a) Work surface speed:} \\
V_w &= \dfrac{\pi d_w N_w}{60} \; \text{m/min} \\
V_w &= \dfrac{3.1416 \times 0.060 \times 120}{1} \quad \text{[since mm·RPM÷60 = m/min when d in mm]} \\
V_w &= 22.62 \; \text{m/min}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{(b) Material removal rate:} \\
\text{MRR} &= V_w \times a \times b \\
\text{where } a = 0.025 \; \text{mm}, \; b = 20 \; \text{mm}, \; V_w = 22.62 \times 1000/60 \; \text{mm/s} \\
V_w \; (\text{mm/s}) &= 22.62 \times \dfrac{1000}{60} = 377 \; \text{mm/s} \\
\text{MRR} &= 377 \times 0.025 \times 20 \\
\text{MRR} &= 188.5 \; \text{mm}^3/\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** $V_w = 22.62$ m/min, **MRR = 188.5 mm³/s**.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Drilling Tool Point Grinding (Clearance Angle Setup)

**Question (5 marks):** A tool and cutter grinder is set up to grind the **point of an HSS drill bit (point angle = 118°)**. The drill shank is held in a collet on a workhead. What is the included angle the workhead should be set to if the point is to be ground on the **periphery of a cup wheel** so that the **cutting lip is horizontal**?

**Solution Logic:**

A standard drill point has a *point angle* of 118° between the two lips. The clearance at the margin equals **half of the difference** between 180° (flat lip) and 118°:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Lip clearance angle} &= \dfrac{180° - 118°}{2} \\
&= \dfrac{62°}{2} \\
&= 31°
\end{aligned}
$$

The workhead is therefore set at **31°** from horizontal (tilted in the direction of the wheel feed).

**Answer:** Workhead setting = **31°**.

> [!NOTE]
> This value (lip clearance) is sometimes called the **"drill point thinning angle"** in tool-room parlance.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example 4 — Vernier Caliper Reading

**Question (3 marks):** A Vernier caliper main scale reading is **24.0 mm**, and the 7th division on the Vernier scale coincides with a main-scale division. If the least count is **0.02 mm**, what is the measured value?

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Measured value} &= \text{MSR} + (\text{VSR} \times \text{LC}) \\
&= 24.0 + (7 \times 0.02) \\
&= 24.0 + 0.14 \\
&= 24.14 \; \text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** 24.14 mm.

---

## 3.6 Worked Example 5 — Micrometer Zero Error & Reading

**Question (4 marks):** On a 0–25 mm outside micrometer, the spindle is closed on a **25 mm slip gauge**. The micrometer reading is **24.97 mm**. Calculate the zero error and the corrected reading for a workpiece measurement of **18.65 mm**.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{True value on slip gauge} &= 25.00 \; \text{mm} \\
\text{Measured value} &= 24.97 \; \text{mm} \\
\text{Zero error} &= \text{Measured} - \text{True} = 24.97 - 25.00 = -0.03 \; \text{mm} \\
\text{Corrected reading} &= \text{Workpiece reading} - \text{Zero error} \\
&= 18.65 - (-0.03) \\
&= 18.68 \; \text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** Zero error = **−0.03 mm (negative)**, corrected reading = **18.68 mm**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign convention:** If micrometer reads *less* than true value → **negative** zero error → **add** to subsequent readings. If it reads *more* → **positive** zero error → **subtract**.

---

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Wheel Selection Decision Tree

| Workpiece Material | Hardness (BHN) | Recommended Abrasive | Grit | Grade | Bond |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Mild Steel | 120–180 | A (Al₂O₃) | 36–60 | K–M | V |
| Hardened Steel | 250–650 | A (Al₂O₃) | 60–80 | H–K | V |
| HSS Tools | 600–900 | A (white Al₂O₃) | 60–80 | H–J | V |
| Cast Iron | 150–250 | C (SiC) | 36–46 | K–M | V |
| Tungsten Carbide | 1400–1800 | D (Diamond) | 120–180 | — | M (Metal) |
| Ceramics (Al₂O₃) | 1500+ | D (Diamond) | 100–220 | — | R / M |
| HRC 60+ alloy | 60+ HRC | CBN | 80–120 | — | V / R |

---

## 3.8 Analytical Matrix — Surface Speed Recommendations (KTU Practical Range)

| Operation | Wheel Surface Speed (m/s) | Work Speed (m/min) | In-feed (mm/pass) | Coolant |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Surface grinding (steel) | 25–30 | 5–15 m/min table | 0.005–0.025 | Flood |
| Cyl. grinding (steel) | 28–35 | 15–30 | 0.005–0.05 | Flood |
| Tool grinding (HSS) | 20–25 | Manual | 0.01–0.02 | Light flood |
| Internal grinding | 15–25 | 20–40 | 0.0025–0.01 | Flood high pressure |

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]
**Q:** List any **six** standard marking characters in a grinding wheel specification as per **ANSI B7.1**, and state the meaning of each in one line.

**Model Answer:**
1. **Abrasive type** (e.g., A — Aluminium Oxide) — the cutting material of the grains.
2. **Grain size** (e.g., 60) — average mesh size of the abrasive.
3. **Grade** (e.g., K) — hardness of the bond holding the grains.
4. **Structure** (e.g., 5) — spacing or porosity of the wheel.
5. **Bond type** (e.g., V) — the matrix binding the grains (V = Vitrified).
6. **Manufacturer's code** (e.g., BF) — vendor-specific symbol.

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]
**Q:** Differentiate between **plunge grinding** and **traverse (through-feed) grinding** in cylindrical grinding. Mention one application of each.

**Model Answer:**
| Feature | Plunge Grinding | Traverse Grinding |
|---|---|---|
| Wheel feed | Radial in-feed only | Radial + axial traverse |
| Workpiece motion | Rotational | Rotational + axial feed |
| Application | Shoulders, stepped shafts, form features | Long uniform shafts (e.g., pins, axles) |
| Productivity | Lower (single pass) | Higher for long parts |
| Setup | Simple | Needs reverse/end-stop arrangement |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [ESE Pattern]

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **constructional features and working principle of a horizontal-spindle surface grinding machine**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (Key Points):**
- **Main parts:** Base, column, saddle, table, wheel head, magnetic chuck, coolant system, dresser.
- **Working principle:** Wheel rotates at high speed (25–35 m/s), table reciprocates under the wheel, workpiece is held on magnetic chuck.
- **Feed systems:** Vertical (down-feed), cross (in-out), longitudinal (table traverse).
- **Sketch needed:** Show wheel above the workpiece, table with magnetic chuck, dresser, coolant nozzle.

**(b)** A surface grinder uses a wheel of diameter **250 mm** at **1440 RPM**. The table speed is **10 m/min** and cross-feed is **10 mm/pass**. Calculate (i) wheel surface speed in m/s, (ii) MRR in mm³/s if depth of cut is **0.02 mm** and width of contact is **25 mm**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{(i) Wheel surface speed: } V_s &= \dfrac{\pi D_w N_w}{60000} \\
V_s &= \dfrac{3.1416 \times 250 \times 1440}{60000} \\
V_s &= 18.85 \; \text{m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{(ii) MRR: convert } V_{table} \text{ to mm/s: } 10 \; \text{m/min} = \dfrac{10 \times 1000}{60} = 166.67 \; \text{mm/s} \\
\text{MRR} &= V_{table} \times a \times b \\
\text{MRR} &= 166.67 \times 0.02 \times 25 \\
\text{MRR} &= 83.33 \; \text{mm}^3/\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answers:** $V_s = 18.85$ m/s; MRR = 83.33 mm³/s.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [ESE Pattern — Alternative Choice]

**(a)** Describe with a sketch the **universal tool and cutter grinder** and explain the procedure for **grinding the point of a twist drill**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (Key Points):**
- **Universal T&C grinder:** has a swiveling workhead, swiveling wheel head, and a versatile table.
- **Drill point grinding procedure:**
  1. Hold the drill shank in a collet.
  2. Set the workhead at the **lip clearance angle** (e.g., 31° for 118° point).
  3. Bring the cutting lip to a horizontal position.
  4. Use a **cup wheel** with grit 60–80, dry or light coolant.
  5. Pivot the workhead back and forth to generate the **flank surface** without burning the cutting edge.
  6. Repeat on the second lip and **check for equal lengths** (lip height gauge).

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the principle of an **outside micrometer (0–25 mm)**. A micrometer reads **24.92 mm** on a 25 mm slip gauge. What is the **zero error**, and what is the **corrected reading** if a shaft measures **18.50 mm**? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Micrometer Principle:** The micrometer works on the **screw-and-nut** principle. The spindle advances **0.5 mm per revolution** (or **1 mm**, depending on thread pitch). The thimble has **50 divisions**, so each division = **0.01 mm**, giving a least count of **0.01 mm**.

**Diagram needed:** Sleeve with main scale (0–25 mm with 0.5 mm sub-divisions), thimble (0–50), ratchet, frame, anvil, spindle.

**Zero error calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{True value} &= 25.00 \; \text{mm} \\
\text{Measured} &= 24.92 \; \text{mm} \\
\text{Zero error} &= 24.92 - 25.00 = -0.08 \; \text{mm (negative)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Corrected reading:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Corrected} &= 18.50 - (-0.08) \\
&= 18.58 \; \text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** Zero error = **−0.08 mm**; Corrected reading = **18.58 mm**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Unit mismatch:** Writing $V_s$ in m/s without converting diameter from mm. KTU examiners *always* deduct for inconsistent units.
> 2. **Forgetting the "×1000" or "÷1000"** when converting between mm, m, and per-minute vs. per-second.
> 3. **Confusing wheel grade with wheel hardness of abrasive.** The grade refers to the **bond hardness**, not the abrasive itself.
> 4. **Skipping spark-out explanation** in surface grinding — a frequently asked viva question worth 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Not labeling the micrometer thimble divisions correctly.** Many students forget the sleeve has 0.5 mm sub-graduations and lose a mark.
> 6. **Zero error sign error** — writing 18.50 − 0.08 = 18.42 instead of 18.50 + 0.08 = 18.58. The examiner will *always* deduct here.
> 7. **Omitting the coolant's role.** Coolant is not just for cooling — it also **lubricates, flushes chips, and prevents thermal damage to the workpiece.**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Grinding is an abrasive machining process** using a multi-point wheel of geometrically undefined cutting edges.
- [x] **Wheel specification** = Abrasive + Grit + Grade + Structure + Bond (ANSI / ISO 525).
- [x] **Rule of selection:** soft wheel → hard material; hard wheel → soft material.
- [x] **Wheel surface speed** $V_s = \dfrac{\pi D_w N_w}{60000}$ m/s.
- [x] **MRR in grinding** $MRR = V_{table} \cdot a \cdot b$ — for surface grinding.
- [x] **Surface grinding** = flat surface, reciprocating table, magnetic chuck.
- [x] **Cylindrical grinding** = external cylindrical surface, between centers.
- [x] **Tool & Cutter Grinder** = sharpens cutting tools (drills, end mills, form tools).
- [x] **Drill point angle** = **118°** (general purpose HSS).
- [x] **Lip clearance** for drill = (180° − point angle) / 2.
- [x] **Vernier caliper LC** = **0.02 mm**; **Micrometer LC** = **0.01 mm**; **DTI LC** = **0.01 mm**.
- [x] **Zero error sign convention:** +ve when reading > true; −ve when reading < true; *always correct the measured value* by **subtracting the zero error**.
- [x] **Coolant** = aqueous emulsion 1:20; functions: cooling, lubrication, chip flushing, preventing thermal damage.
- [x] **Spark-out passes** = finishing passes with no in-feed for improved surface finish.
- [x] **Safety:** Always check wheel for cracks by "ring test," use wheel guard, wear eye protection, never exceed rated RPM.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Study Tip:** For the KTU viva, always be ready to (a) identify the **type of wheel** from a sample marking, (b) compute **$V_s$** in m/s, (c) explain **spark analysis** (e.g., yellow sparks with bursts = medium-carbon steel; long blue sparks with no bursts = low-carbon steel), and (d) state the **least count** of any instrument in the lab.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Exercises on Welding: Arc and Gas Welding, Butt Welding, and Lap Welding of M.S. Sheets
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUL407 - MANUFACTURING AND PROTOTYPING LABORATORY
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Exercises on Welding: Arc and Gas Welding, Butt Welding, and Lap Welding of M.S. Sheets

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Welding — The Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Welding** is a **permanent joining process** in which two or more pieces of metal are united by heating them to a suitable temperature, with or without the application of pressure and with or without the use of a filler material, to produce a localized union across the interface. The bond relies on **atomic-level metallurgical fusion** rather than mechanical interlocking.

For the scope of this experiment, the KTU 2024 Scheme restricts focus to **fusion welding** of **Mild Steel (M.S.) sheets**, specifically:

| Welding Process | Energy Source | KTU Category |
|---|---|---|
| **Arc Welding (SMAW)** | Electric Arc | Electric Resistance / Arc Family |
| **Gas Welding (Oxy-Acetylene)** | Combustion of C₂H₂ + O₂ | Chemical / Gas Family |
| **Butt Joint** | A *joint configuration*, not a process | Square / V-groove geometry |
| **Lap Joint** | A *joint configuration*, not a process | Overlap geometry |

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of welding like repairing a broken chocolate bar with melted chocolate.**

Imagine a chocolate bar snaps in two. If you simply press the two halves together, they may touch but will not become *one piece*. However, if you melt a little chocolate, paint it on the broken ends, and press them together while it cools — the two halves become a single, continuous bar. The melted chocolate acts as the "filler," and the heat enables the molecules to intermingle.

In welding:
- The **broken chocolate** = the M.S. sheets to be joined
- The **melted chocolate** = molten filler metal (from electrode rod or filler wire)
- The **heat source** = the electric arc (Arc Welding) or the gas flame (Gas Welding)
- The **pressing** = metallurgical pressure / surface tension-driven coalescence

The **arc** is like a tiny, focused lightning bolt (~6000 °C) that melts metal almost instantly. The **gas flame** is like a very hot candle flame (~3200 °C) that melts metal more slowly and is easier to control.

## 1.3 Why Mild Steel (M.S.) Sheets?

M.S. sheets are the KTU-preferred specimen because they:
- Have a **carbon content of 0.15%–0.25%** — low enough to weld easily without cracking.
- Are **cheap, abundant, and dimensionally uniform** (typically **1.6 mm to 6 mm** thick for lab use).
- Show **visible weld bead** and **clear distortion / color change** — ideal for student observation.
- Do not require pre-heating or post-heating for thin gauges.

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Arc Temperature (SMAW):** **6,000 °C to 7,000 °C** at the arc core.
> - **Oxy-Acetylene Flame Temperature:** **3,200 °C to 3,500 °C** (neutral flame).
> - **Melting Point of M.S.:** **~1,500 °C**.
> - **Acetylene Combustion Reaction:** $\text{C}_2\text{H}_2 + \tfrac{5}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{Heat}$
> - **Standard Lab Sheet Thickness (KTU):** **2 mm, 3 mm, or 4 mm M.S. sheet**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Arc Welding (Shielded Metal Arc Welding — SMAW)

> [!NOTE]
> **SMAW** is officially called *Manual Metal Arc (MMA) welding* in Indian standards (IS 813). It is the most common process taught in KTU workshops because the equipment is rugged and inexpensive.

### 2.1.1 Operating Principle
- An **electric arc** is struck between a **consumable electrode** (positive terminal — anode) and the **workpiece** (negative terminal — cathode).
- The arc melts both the **electrode tip** (forming the filler metal) and the **base metal** at the joint.
- The electrode's **flux coating** decomposes to release shielding gas and forms a protective **slag layer** that prevents atmospheric contamination.

### 2.1.2 Why and How of the Arc
- **Why?** The arc concentrates enormous energy in a tiny spot — current density at the arc root can exceed **10⁴ A/cm²**, producing local temperatures far above the melting point of steel.
- **How?** The electrode is touched to the workpiece and then withdrawn by **2 mm to 4 mm**. The ionized air gap conducts current, sustaining the arc.

### 2.1.3 Process Parameters
1. **Open Circuit Voltage (OCV):** **50 V to 80 V** — supplied by the welding transformer.
2. **Arc Voltage:** **18 V to 30 V** — measured across the arc.
3. **Welding Current:** **40 A to 200 A**, chosen by the rule of thumb:
$$I = (25 \text{ to } 40) \times d \;\; (\text{in Amperes, where } d = \text{electrode diameter in mm})$$
4. **Electrode Diameter:** Usually **2.5 mm, 3.15 mm, or 4 mm**.
5. **Electrode Type for M.S.:** **E6013** (rutile-coated, all-position, AC/DC).

### 2.1.4 Polarity Conventions

| Polarity | Connection | Heat Distribution | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **DCEP (Electrode +)** | Electrode → +ve, Work → −ve | 2/3 in workpiece | High penetration, M.S. sheets |
| **DCEN (Electrode −)** | Electrode → −ve, Work → +ve | 2/3 in electrode | Thin sheets, surfacing |
| **AC** | Alternating | Balanced | General purpose, E6013 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU lab M.S. sheets, use **DCEP with E6013, 3.15 mm electrode, ~90 A current**.

## 2.2 Gas Welding (Oxy-Acetylene Welding — OAW)

### 2.2.1 Operating Principle
- **Acetylene (C₂H₂)** stored in a cylinder dissolves in **acetone** for safe transport.
- **Oxygen (O₂)** in a separate cylinder supports combustion.
- Gases flow through **regulators** → **hoses** → **torch**, where they mix and burn at the **nozzle tip**.

### 2.2.2 The Three Flames (High-Yield KTU Question!)

> [!CAUTION]
> Examiner's favourite: *"Differentiate the three oxy-acetylene flames."*

| Flame Type | O₂ : C₂H₂ Ratio | Cone Length | Appearance | Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Carburizing (Reducing)** | < 1.0 | Long, ragged | Feathered white | Hard-facing, brass |
| **Neutral** | **1.0 to 1.2** | Sharp, defined | Bright bluish inner cone | **M.S., C.S., CI, Cu — General purpose** |
| **Oxidizing** | > 1.5 | Short, pointed | Hissing, harsh | Brass, bronze (NOT steel) |

### 2.2.3 Why a Neutral Flame for M.S.?
A neutral flame neither adds carbon (carburizing) nor burns out carbon (oxidizing). Mild steel has a balanced composition; any chemical imbalance leads to **porosity, hardness, or brittleness** in the weld.

### 2.2.4 Process Parameters
- **Oxygen Pressure:** **0.7 to 2.0 kg/cm²** (regulator setting).
- **Acetylene Pressure:** **0.3 to 0.7 kg/cm²**.
- **Nozzle Size:** **No. 1 to No. 3** for 1–4 mm M.S. sheet.
- **Filler Rod:** Mild steel rod of matching diameter, coated with flux (e.g., borax-based).

### 2.2.5 Welder's Hand Technique
- **Forehand (Rightward) Welding:** Flame and rod move in the same direction — used for **thin sheets** (≤ 3 mm).
- **Backhand (Leftward) Welding:** Flame points back at the weld pool — used for **thicker plates** with deep penetration.

## 2.3 Butt Joint of M.S. Sheets

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **butt joint** is a configuration in which two members lie in the same plane and meet edge-to-edge.

| Sheet Thickness | Edge Preparation | Gap | Root Face |
|---|---|---|---|
| ≤ 2 mm | **Square butt** (no groove) | 0–1 mm | Nil |
| 3–6 mm | **V-groove** (60°–70° included angle) | 1.5–2.5 mm | 1–1.5 mm |

- **Tack welds** are placed at both ends before final welding to maintain gap.
- For **arc welding**, run a **root pass** then a **cap pass** for thicker sheets.
- For **gas welding**, use **leftward technique** with slight weaving.

## 2.4 Lap Joint of M.S. Sheets

> [!NOTE]
> A **lap joint** is formed by overlapping two pieces over a distance of **2T to 5T** (where T = sheet thickness), then welding along the edges of the overlap.

- **Fillet welds** form on both sides of the lap — one continuous, one with possible penetration.
- **Advantages over butt:** No edge preparation, self-aligning, larger weld area, higher strength.
- **Disadvantages:** Adds thickness, material wastage, stress concentration at the overlap edge.

## 2.5 KTU Formula / Parameter Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Use `\vert` instead of `|` to avoid breaking markdown tables. The following table is the **high-yield reference** for viva and written exams.

| Parameter | Symbol | Formula / Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Welding current selection | $I$ | $I = 30 \cdot d_{electrode}$ | A |
| Heat input (arc) | $H$ | $H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v \cdot 1000}$ | kJ/mm |
| Arc efficiency (SMAW) | $\eta$ | $\eta = 0.7 \text{ to } 0.85$ | — |
| Welding speed | $v$ | $v = \dfrac{60 \cdot L}{t}$ | mm/min |
| Neutral flame O₂:C₂H₂ ratio | $R$ | $R = 1 \text{ to } 1.2$ | — |
| Lap overlap length | $L_{lap}$ | $L_{lap} = 2T \text{ to } 5T$ | mm |
| Fillet weld leg size | $z$ | $z = 0.707 \cdot t_{throat}$ | mm |
| Throat thickness | $t_{throat}$ | $t_{throat} = 0.707 \cdot z$ | mm |
| Shear strength of fillet | $\tau$ | $\tau = 0.707 \cdot z \cdot L \cdot \sigma_{allow}$ | N |

**Notation:** $V$ = arc voltage (V), $I$ = current (A), $v$ = travel speed (mm/s), $L$ = weld length (mm), $t$ = time (s), $T$ = sheet thickness.

## 2.6 Real-World Utility of These Processes

- **SMAW** is the global workhorse for *structural fabrication, shipbuilding, pipeline welding in the field* — its portability is unmatched.
- **Oxy-Acetylene** remains vital for *pipe fitting, repair in remote sites, brazing, soldering, and heating for bending* — it is also the only welding process that can *cut* thick steel.
- **Butt joints** are the primary joint in *boilers, pressure vessels, ship hulls*.
- **Lap joints** dominate *automotive chassis, sheet metal enclosures, and air ducting*.
