# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Selecting Welding Current from Electrode Diameter

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This derivation is *the most frequently asked Part-A calculation* in KTU lab exams.

**Given:** Electrode diameter $d = 3.15$ mm, mild steel base metal, E6013 rutile-coated electrode.

**Step 1 — Identify the empirical rule of thumb used in industry:**

For a **rutile (E6013)** electrode, the recommended current density is $j = 25 \text{ to } 35$ A/mm². We pick the standard KTU textbook formula:

$$I = k \cdot d$$

where the constant $k = 30$ A/mm is used for *general-purpose SMAW* on M.S.

**Step 2 — Substitute the electrode diameter:**

$$I = 30 \times 3.15$$

**Step 3 — Compute the numerical value:**

$$I = 94.5 \text{ A}$$

**Step 4 — Round to the practical tap setting on the transformer:**

$$I \approx 95 \text{ A (practical setting)}$$

**Step 5 — State the acceptable range:**

$$I_{min} = 25 \times 3.15 = 78.75 \text{ A}$$
$$I_{max} = 40 \times 3.15 = 126 \text{ A}$$

The chosen value of **95 A** lies inside the safe envelope. ✅

## 3.2 Derivation: Heat Input in Arc Welding (and Why It Matters)

**Why heat input matters:** Excessive heat input causes **wider HAZ (Heat Affected Zone), grain growth, distortion, and reduced toughness**. Insufficient heat causes **lack of fusion, porosity, and slag inclusion**.

**Step 1 — Define heat input.**
The thermal energy deposited per unit length of weld is:

$$H = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v \cdot 1000} \;\; \text{(kJ/mm)}$$

where $V$ is in volts, $I$ in amperes, $v$ in mm/s.

**Step 2 — Insert values for a typical KTU lab pass.**
Take $V = 22$ V, $I = 95$ A, $v = 3$ mm/s, $\eta = 0.80$.

**Step 3 — Substitute into the equation.**

$$H = \frac{0.80 \times 22 \times 95}{3 \times 1000}$$

**Step 4 — Simplify the numerator.**

$$H = \frac{0.80 \times 22 \times 95}{3000} = \frac{1672}{3000}$$

**Step 5 — Final value.**

$$H = 0.557 \text{ kJ/mm}$$

**Step 6 — Compare to the safe limit for M.S. sheets (≤ 6 mm).**
Recommended: $H \le 1.0$ kJ/mm. Since $0.557 < 1.0$, the procedure is **safe and distortion-controlled**. ✅

## 3.3 Derivation: Fillet Weld Strength of a Lap Joint

**Why this is the "Part-B favourite":** A lap joint carries load through **shear** across the *throat* of the fillet weld.

**Step 1 — Geometry of a fillet weld.**
For an equal-leg fillet of leg size $z$, the **throat thickness** is:

$$t_{throat} = z \cdot \cos(45°) = 0.707 \cdot z$$

**Step 2 — Effective area resisting shear (per unit length).**

$$A_{eff} = 0.707 \cdot z \cdot 1 \;\; \text{(per mm length)}$$

**Step 3 — Allowable shear load per mm length.**
Let the allowable shear stress of the weld metal be $\tau_{allow}$.

$$F_{per\,mm} = 0.707 \cdot z \cdot \tau_{allow}$$

**Step 4 — Total load capacity for weld length $L$.**

$$F_{total} = 0.707 \cdot z \cdot L \cdot \tau_{allow}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical check (KTU typical).**
Let $z = 4$ mm, $L = 80$ mm (lap length), $\tau_{allow} = 110$ N/mm² (E6013 weld metal).

**Step 6 — Substitute.**

$$F_{total} = 0.707 \times 4 \times 80 \times 110$$

**Step 7 — Simplify.**

$$F_{total} = 0.707 \times 4 \times 8800 = 0.707 \times 35200$$

**Step 8 — Final result.**

$$F_{total} \approx 24{,}886 \text{ N} \approx 24.9 \text{ kN}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **single-pass, single-side fillet** strength. A two-sided lap weld would carry ≈ 49.8 kN.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Defects vs. Causes vs. Remedies

> [!WARNING]
> The KTU examiner loves to ask: *"Your weld specimen has porosity — what went wrong?"* Use this matrix to answer.

| Defect | Visual Cue | Probable Cause | Corrective Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Porosity** | Small cavities, "worm holes" | Dirty metal, wet electrode, long arc | Clean base metal, dry electrode, short arc |
| **Lack of fusion** | Sharp boundary, no melting at interface | Low current, fast travel | Increase current, slow down |
| **Lack of penetration** | Weld doesn't reach root | Low current, wrong gap | Increase current, set proper root gap |
| **Undercut** | Groove at weld toe | Excessive current, long arc | Reduce current, use correct angle |
| **Spatter** | Droplets on surface | Long arc, high current, damp electrode | Short arc, lower current, dry electrode |
| **Crater crack** | Star-shaped crack at end | Arc broken abruptly | Use crater-filling technique (retrace) |
| **Distortion** | Sheet bends / warps | Uneven heating, poor clamping | Use tack welds, back-step sequence, clamping |
| **Slag inclusion** | Non-metallic flakes inside weld | Poor cleaning between passes | Chip and brush between passes |
| **Excess penetration** | Weld sags through root | High current, no backing | Use backing strip, reduce current |
| **Overlap** | Weld metal rolls over base | Too slow travel, too low current | Increase travel speed or current |

## 3.5 Derivation: Gas Flow for Oxy-Acetylene Torch

**Step 1 — Stoichiometric ratio from combustion.**
From $\text{C}_2\text{H}_2 + \tfrac{5}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$, the **stoichiometric** ratio is:

$$R_{stoich} = \frac{5}{2} = 2.5$$

**Step 2 — Practical neutral ratio.**
In practice, the ratio is given as $1 : 1$ to $1.2 : 1$ by *volume*. This is because **pure oxygen is supplied in excess** of the combustion requirement, and the torch injects it directly — the extra oxygen isn't *all* consumed.

**Step 3 — Convert to practical mass ratio.**
Density of O₂ ≈ 1.43 kg/m³; density of C₂H₂ ≈ 1.17 kg/m³. For neutral flame:

$$R_{mass} = \frac{1 \times 1.43}{1 \times 1.17} = 1.22 \text{ kg O}_2 \text{ per kg C}_2\text{H}_2$$

**Step 4 — Estimate cylinder life.**
If acetylene flow = 5 L/min and cylinder volume = 7 m³ (7,000 L):

$$t = \frac{7000}{5} = 1400 \text{ min} = 23.3 \text{ h}$$

> [!NOTE]
> In a 2-hour lab session, one acetylene cylinder is sufficient for ~10 students.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — 3 Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Question 1
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between arc welding and gas welding in any three aspects.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — keyword style):**

| Aspect | Arc Welding (SMAW) | Gas Welding (OAW) |
|---|---|---|
| **Heat Source** | Electric arc at 6,000–7,000 °C | Combustion of acetylene with oxygen at 3,200 °C |
| **Filler Material** | Consumable electrode rod itself | Separate filler rod fed manually |
| **Shielding** | Flux coating decomposes to form gas + slag | Excess oxygen from flame itself (no flux for M.S.) |
| **Portability** | Requires power source; cables limit mobility | Cylinders + torch — fully portable |
| **Suitability for M.S. Sheet** | Excellent; fast, deep penetration | Excellent for thin sheets (< 3 mm) |
| **Skill Required** | Moderate (electrode angle, arc length) | High (flame adjustment + filler feeding) |

*(Any three differences with valid technical terms — full 3 marks.)*

---

### Question 2
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List any six safety precautions to be observed during arc welding in the laboratory.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — ½ mark each):**
1. Wear **welding helmet with proper filter lens (shade 10–13)** to protect eyes from UV/IR radiation.
2. Use **flame-resistant leather gloves and apron** to prevent burns from spatter.
3. Ensure **proper earthing** of the workpiece to prevent stray current shocks.
4. Provide **adequate ventilation / exhaust fan** to remove toxic fumes (especially when welding galvanised or coated steel).
5. Keep a **fire extinguisher (CO₂ or dry powder)** within 2 m of the welding station.
6. Use **welding screens / curtains** to protect other students from arc flash.
7. Inspect cables, holder, and earth clamp for **insulation damage** before use.

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14 Mark Module-Internal Choice Questions

### Question A — 14 Marks
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2]`

**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the **oxy-acetylene gas welding process**. List the equipment used and explain the procedure to produce a **butt joint on 3 mm M.S. sheet**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **three types of oxy-acetylene flames** with a labelled sketch. Justify why a **neutral flame is preferred for welding M.S. sheets**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

*Sketch description (3 marks):* Draw the cylinder assembly:
- Two cylinders — **O₂ (black body)** and **C₂H₂ (maroon body)** — each fitted with a **regulator** (two-gauge type: cylinder pressure + working pressure).
- Two colour-coded **hoses** — **Blue for Oxygen, Red for Acetylene** — connected to a **blowpipe/torch** with a **mixing chamber** and a **nozzle tip** (No. 1, 2, or 3).

*Equipment list (2 marks):*
1. Oxygen cylinder + regulator
2. Acetylene cylinder + regulator
3. Hoses (O₂ = blue, C₂H₂ = red)
4. Torch with nozzle
5. Filler rod (M.S. coated)
6. Spark lighter / friction lighter
7. Tongs, wire brush, chipping hammer, PPE

*Procedure (2 marks):*
1. Check cylinder pressures (O₂ ≈ 100–150 kg/cm², C₂H₂ ≈ 15–25 kg/cm²).
2. Open cylinder valves slowly; set working pressures: O₂ ≈ 1 kg/cm², C₂H₂ ≈ 0.5 kg/cm².
3. Prepare 3 mm M.S. sheets — clean with wire brush, set square edges, maintain **root gap of 1.5–2 mm**, fix with **tack welds at both ends**.
4. Light acetylene first, then slowly introduce oxygen; adjust to **neutral flame** (sharp inner cone, ~2× length of nozzle).
5. Hold torch at **45° forehand**; melt base metal and dip filler rod into the pool.
6. Move steadily along the joint; finish by releasing acetylene first, then oxygen.
7. Cool, chip slag, brush, and inspect bead.

---

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

*Three flames (4 marks):*

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph C[Carburizing Flame]
        C1[O2 to C2H2 ratio less than 1.0]
        C2[Excess acetylene - adds carbon]
        C3[Long feathered white inner cone]
        C4[Use - hardfacing - brass welding]
    end
    subgraph N[Neutral Flame]
        N1[O2 to C2H2 ratio 1.0 to 1.2]
        N2[Complete combustion - no chemical change]
        N3[Sharp bright bluish inner cone]
        N4[Use - MS - CS - CI - copper - GENERAL]
    end
    subgraph O[Oxidizing Flame]
        O1[O2 to C2H2 ratio greater than 1.5]
        O2[Excess oxygen - burns out carbon]
        O3[Short pointed cone with hissing sound]
        O4[Use - brass and bronze only]
    end
```

*Why neutral for M.S.? (3 marks):*
- Mild steel has **0.15%–0.25% C**. A **carburizing** flame would push carbon into the weld → **hard, brittle HAZ** prone to cracking.
- An **oxidizing** flame would burn out carbon and leave **porous, oxide-filled, weak weld metal**.
- A **neutral flame** maintains the original chemistry — no carbon pickup, no carbon loss → **ductile, tough, sound weld**.
- Therefore, the neutral flame is the standard choice for M.S. in KTU lab exercises.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2]`

**(a)** Describe **Shielded Metal Arc Welding (SMAW)** with a labelled diagram. List the variables that affect weld quality, and state the **function of the flux coating** on the electrode. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **butt joint and lap joint** for M.S. sheet welding. For a lap joint of 4 mm M.S. sheets with leg size $z = 5$ mm and weld length $L = 100$ mm, calculate the **shear load capacity** if the allowable shear stress is 110 N/mm². **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

*Process description (3 marks):*
SMAW uses a **consumable flux-coated electrode** held in an electrode holder connected to one terminal of a welding transformer/rectifier. The workpiece is connected to the other terminal via an earth clamp. When the electrode is touched to the workpiece and withdrawn by ~3 mm, an **electric arc** is established, generating temperatures of **6,000–7,000 °C**. The arc melts the electrode tip and the base metal, forming a common weld pool. The flux coating vaporises, releasing **shielding gas** that displaces atmospheric N₂ and O₂. On cooling, a **slag layer** covers the bead and is later chipped off.

*Variables affecting weld quality (2 marks):*
- **Welding current** (A) — too low = lack of fusion; too high = burn-through
- **Arc voltage / arc length** — long arc = porosity; short arc = stable, sound weld
- **Travel speed** — too fast = lack of penetration; too slow = excess heat, distortion
- **Electrode angle** — affects bead shape and penetration
- **Electrode type and diameter**
- **Surface cleanliness** of base metal

*Function of flux coating (2 marks):*
1. Generates **shielding gas** to protect the weld pool from atmospheric gases.
2. Forms a **molten slag** that floats on the weld, slowing cooling and protecting the bead.
3. Adds **deoxidising elements (Si, Mn)** to clean the weld metal.
4. Provides **arc-stabilising compounds** (e.g., rutile TiO₂ in E6013) for smooth arc.
5. Improves weld bead **shape and appearance**.

---

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

*Comparison table (3 marks):*

| Aspect | Butt Joint | Lap Joint |
|---|---|---|
| **Edge preparation** | Required (square/V-groove) | None — just overlap |
| **Weld type** | Groove weld (root + cap) | Fillet welds on one or both sides |
| **Strength** | Full base-metal strength possible | Limited by weld throat |
| **Material use** | Efficient | Wastes 2T–5T of length |
| **Distortion** | Higher | Lower |
| **Inspection** | Easier (NDT friendly) | Harder — overlapping region |
| **Typical use** | Boilers, vessels, pipes | Sheet metal, automotive, frames |

*Calculation (4 marks):*

**Step 1 — Throat thickness formula:**

$$t_{throat} = 0.707 \cdot z$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $z = 5$ mm:**

$$t_{throat} = 0.707 \times 5 = 3.535 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Effective shear area for length L = 100 mm:**

$$A_{eff} = 0.707 \cdot z \cdot L = 3.535 \times 100 = 353.5 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 4 — Load capacity using $\tau_{allow} = 110$ N/mm²:**

$$F = \tau_{allow} \cdot A_{eff} = 110 \times 353.5$$

**Step 5 — Final answer:**

$$F = 38{,}885 \text{ N} \approx 38.9 \text{ kN}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Mark-Loss Zones:**
> 1. **Forgetting the 0.707 factor** for throat thickness → loses 1 mark.
> 2. **Using leg size `z` as the area's effective dimension** → loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Mixing up butt and lap definitions** in the comparison → loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Forgetting to mention flux function** in SMAW (more than just "shielding") → loses ½–1 mark.
> 5. **Stating lap joint strength = base metal strength** — this is **false**; it is limited by throat.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for the KTU Viva + Written Exam**

- ✅ Welding is a **permanent fusion** joint; fasteners (rivets, bolts) are *not* welding.
- ✅ **SMAW** = arc welding = consumable electrode + flux coating + slag protection.
- ✅ **OAW** = oxy-acetylene = **O₂ + C₂H₂**, **neutral flame** has **O₂ : C₂H₂ = 1 : 1 to 1.2 : 1**.
- ✅ Arc temperature: **6,000–7,000 °C**; flame temperature: **~3,200 °C**.
- ✅ Standard KTU lab electrode: **E6013, 3.15 mm, DCEP, ~90–100 A** for 3 mm M.S. sheet.
- ✅ Welding current rule: $I = 30 \cdot d$ (A, with $d$ in mm) — a **must-memorise** formula.
- ✅ Heat input: $H = \dfrac{\eta V I}{v \cdot 1000}$ in **kJ/mm** — keep **< 1.0 kJ/mm** for thin M.S. sheets.
- ✅ **Butt joint** = same plane, edge-to-edge; **Lap joint** = overlap of **2T to 5T**.
- ✅ **Fillet weld throat** = **0.707 × leg size**; shear load = $0.707 \cdot z \cdot L \cdot \tau_{allow}$.
- ✅ **Three flames** to remember: **Carburising** (long, white, carbon-adds), **Neutral** (sharp, balanced, M.S. use), **Oxidising** (short, hissing, brass only).
- ✅ **Six common defects**: Porosity, Lack of fusion, Lack of penetration, Undercut, Spatter, Distortion — each has a *cause* and a *cure*; remember at least three pairs.
- ✅ **Hose colours**: **Oxygen = Blue**, **Acetylene = Red** — safety-critical and **frequently asked**.
- ✅ Always **dry the electrode** (150 °C, 1 h) before use to avoid porosity.
- ✅ Always **chip slag and wire-brush** between multi-pass welds.
- ✅ **Back-step technique** reduces distortion in long butt welds.
- ✅ Neutral flame **inner cone length ≈ 2× nozzle tip length** — visual check.
- ✅ KTU lab record must include: **process parameters, sketch, defect list, observations, photograph of bead**.
- ✅ **Forehand** (rightward) for thin sheets; **backhand** (leftward) for thick plates in gas welding.
- ✅ For lap joints, weld **both sides** if possible — it roughly **doubles** the strength.

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**CNC Lathe (Computer Numerical Control Lathe)** is a precision automated machine tool in which the relative motion between the workpiece (rotating about the spindle axis) and the single-point cutting tool is controlled by a sequence of alphanumeric instructions (G-code/M-code) executed by a dedicated micro-processor-based controller. In the KTU 2024 scheme laboratory syllabus (PCRUL407), the CNC turning centre is treated as a two-axis contouring system whose **X-axis** controls the cross-slide (radial/diameter) and **Z-axis** controls the carriage (longitudinal) motion.

**Part Programming** is the art and engineering of writing the sequence of machining instructions in standard ISO/EIA word-address format so that the controller commands the slides, spindle, coolant, and tool changer to produce a geometry within specified tolerances.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus (Module Experiment 2):** The student must be able to write, simulate, dry-run, and verify part programs for **Plain Turning, Step Turning, Taper Turning, Thread Cutting (using G32/G92/G76), and Circular Interpolation (using G02/G03)** on Fanuc/Siemens-style controllers, and validate the tool path on licensed simulation software (e.g., CNC Simulator Pro, Denford ORAC, or FANUC CNC Guide).

### 1.2 Axes Convention on a CNC Lathe

The standard KTU/Fanuc 2-axis turning centre uses:

| Axis | Direction of Travel | Sign Convention | Physical Control |
|------|--------------------|-----------------|-----------------|
| **X** | Cross-slide (radial) | Diameter programming, X+ = away from centre, X– = towards centre | Cross-slide (saddle transverse) |
| **Z** | Carriage (longitudinal) | Z+ = away from chuck, Z– = towards chuck | Longitudinal carriage |

> [!NOTE]
> **X-axis on a CNC lathe is always programmed as the DIAMETER, not the radius.** This is a unique characteristic of turning centres — radial motion is commanded as total diameter because the operator measures and inspects diameters, not radii.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Robotic Potter"

Imagine a skilled potter shaping a clay pot on a rotating wheel. The potter's:
- **Foot pedal (speed control)** = Spindle speed override (G97, G96, S-word).
- **Right hand moving along the axis of rotation (Z)** = Longitudinal feed along Z.
- **Left hand moving inward and outward (X)** = Radial infeed controlling diameter.
- **Memory of the shape sequence** = Part program stored in the CNC controller.
- **Eye-hand coordination (visual feedback)** = Closed-loop feedback from linear encoders / spindle encoder.

A CNC lathe replaces the potter's memory and hands with a numerical controller executing step-by-step instructions. Where the potter improvises, the CNC lathe executes *exactly* what is written — **"Garbage In = Garbage Out (GIGO)"**.

### 1.4 Anatomy of a CNC Lathe Word-Address Block

A typical Fanuc block follows the structure:

$$
\text{N}\,\text{(sequence no.)}\;\;\text{G}\,\text{(preparatory)}\;\;\text{X}\,\text{(diameter)}\;\;\text{Z}\,\text{(length)}\;\;\text{F}\,\text{(feed)}\;\;\text{S}\,\text{(speed)}\;\;\text{T}\,\text{(tool)}\;\;\text{M}\,\text{(miscellaneous)}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Modal vs Non-Modal Codes:**
> - **Modal codes** remain active until cancelled (e.g., G00, G01, G97, G21).
> - **Non-modal (one-shot) codes** execute only in the block where they appear (e.g., G04 dwell, M30 end of program).

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Coordinate System and Datum Setting

On a CNC lathe, the **Machine Zero (M)** is fixed by the manufacturer — typically at the spindle centreline (for X) and at the chuck face / spindle nose (for Z). The **Workpiece Zero / Program Zero (W)** is the origin of the part program, usually set at the **right-hand face centre of the finished workpiece** (Fanuc convention).

The **third axis C** (around the spindle) and the optional **Y-axis** are not addressed in KTU Module 2.

### 2.2 Spindle Speed and Feed Calculations

The following fundamental turning formulae are essential for writing programs with proper machining parameters.

**Cutting Speed (V_c):** The surface speed at the cutting edge, in m/min.

$$
V_c \;=\; \dfrac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{1000}
$$

where:
- $D$ = instantaneous diameter of cut (mm)
- $N$ = spindle speed (rev/min)

**Spindle Speed (N):**

$$
N \;=\; \dfrac{1000 \cdot V_c}{\pi \cdot D}
$$

**Feed Rate (F) in mm/rev:** The axial advance of the tool per spindle revolution.

$$
f \;=\; \text{mm/rev (programmed with G99)}
$$

**Table Feed / Feedrate per minute (mm/min):**

$$
f_m \;=\; f \cdot N
$$

**Metal Removal Rate (MRR):**

$$
\text{MRR} \;=\; f \cdot a_p \cdot V_c \quad \text{(mm}^3\text{/min)}
$$

where $a_p$ = depth of cut (mm).

### 2.3 Taper Geometry — The Half-Angle Formula

For a taper turning between two diameters $D_1$ (start) and $D_2$ (end) over an axial length $L$:

$$
\tan\!\left(\dfrac{\alpha}{2}\right) \;=\; \dfrac{D_1 - D_2}{2 L}
$$

End-point coordinates for a taper cut starting from $(X_1, Z_1)$:

$$
X_2 \;=\; X_1 - (D_1 - D_2)
$$
$$
Z_2 \;=\; Z_1 - L
$$

### 2.4 Thread Geometry — Lead and Pitch

For single-start threads, **Lead = Pitch (P)**. The feed-rate word for a thread-cutting block must equal the pitch:

$$
F \;=\; P \quad \text{(mm/rev)}
$$

Thread depth (radial) for ISO metric profile:

$$
h_1 \;=\; 0.6134 \cdot P
$$

Major diameter: $d$
Minor diameter: $d_2 = d - 2 h_1$

### 2.5 Circular Interpolation — Arc End Points and Radius

For an arc between $(X_1, Z_1)$ and $(X_2, Z_2)$ with radius $R$:

$$
R \;=\; \dfrac{\sqrt{(X_2 - X_1)^2 + (Z_2 - Z_1)^2}}{2 \sin(\theta/2)}
$$

where $\theta$ is the included angle. Equivalently, given $R$, the chord coordinates must satisfy the geometric condition. Fanuc accepts either **R-word** (radius) or **I-, K-word** (incremental centre).

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Formula / Code | Units | Engineering Use |
|-----------|---------------|-------|-----------------|
| Spindle Speed | $N = \dfrac{1000 V_c}{\pi D}$ | rev/min | S-word in program |
| Cutting Speed | $V_c = \dfrac{\pi D N}{1000}$ | m/min | Material selection |
| Feed per rev | $f = \dfrac{F}{N}$ (when G99) | mm/rev | Surface finish control |
| Feed per min | $f_m = f \cdot N$ | mm/min | Material removal rate |
| MRR | $\text{MRR} = f \cdot a_p \cdot V_c$ | mm³/min | Productivity |
| Taper half-angle | $\tan(\alpha/2) = \dfrac{D_1 - D_2}{2 L}$ | rad / deg | Taper turning |
| Thread pitch = lead | $F = P$ | mm | Threading cycles |
| Thread depth | $h_1 = 0.6134 P$ | mm | Threading passes |
| Arc radius (R-format) | $R = \dfrac{\Delta X^2 + \Delta Z^2}{2 \sin\theta}$ | mm | G02/G03 |
| Absolute / Incremental | G90 / G91 | mode | Coordinate mode |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common Material Cutting Speeds (reference):**
> - Mild Steel: $V_c$ ≈ 150–200 m/min
> - Aluminium: $V_c$ ≈ 300–600 m/min
> - Cast Iron: $V_c$ ≈ 80–120 m/min
> - Stainless Steel: $V_c$ ≈ 60–100 m/min

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

CNC turning is the backbone of:
- **Automotive** — crankshafts, brake drums, axle shafts.
- **Aerospace** — blisks, landing-gear pins, hydraulic manifolds.
- **Medical** — titanium bone screws, surgical instrument shafts.
- **General Manufacturing** — bushings, fasteners, threaded studs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why learn manual part-programming in the simulation era?** Because CAM-generated code (Mastercam, Fusion 360, NX) ultimately compiles to the same ISO G/M-code instructions. Understanding block-by-block logic enables the engineer to debug CAM post-processors and optimize roughing/finishing strategies for cycle time reduction.

## 3. Step-by-Step Programs & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Conventions used throughout: **G21** metric, **G40** tool-nose comp cancel, **G99** feed-per-rev, **G97** spindle RPM constant, **G90** absolute. Tool T0101 = turning tool (CNMG insert), T0303 = threading tool (60° profile).

### 3.1 Plain (Straight) Turning

**Workpiece:** Ø40 mm × 60 mm MS bar. Reduce to Ø36 mm over 50 mm length, face the front face, then part off.

```
%
O1001 (PLAIN TURNING - DEMO PROGRAM)
N10  G21 G40 G99 G97
N20  G28 U0 W0          (HOME REFERENCE)
N30  T0101              (TURNING TOOL CHANGE)
N40  M03 S1500          (SPINDLE CW @ 1500 RPM)
N50  G00 X42.0 Z2.0     (RAPID APPROACH)
N60  G01 Z0 F0.15       (TOUCH FACE)
N70  G01 X-1.0 F0.08    (FACE FRONT FACE)
N80  G00 X42.0 Z2.0     (RETRACT)
N90  G01 X36.0          (RADIAL INFEED TO Ø36)
N100 G01 Z-50.0 F0.2    (PLAIN TURNING Ø36 × 50mm)
N110 G00 X42.0          (RADIAL RETRACT)
N120 G00 Z2.0           (LONGITUDINAL RETURN)
N130 G28 U0 W0          (GO HOME)
N140 M05                (SPINDLE STOP)
N150 M30                (PROGRAM END, REWIND)
%
```

**Block-by-block logic:**

- **N10** sets the controller to metric, cancels any lingering tool-nose compensation, sets feed-per-rev (G99), and constant-RPM mode (G97).
- **N20** sends the tool to the machine reference (home) — always a safety first step.
- **N30** rotates the turret to position 1 and selects offset register 1.
- **N40** starts the spindle clockwise at 1500 RPM (chosen using $N = \dfrac{1000 \times 180}{\pi \times 36} \approx 1592 \Rightarrow$ round to 1500).
- **N50** rapid positioning to a safe approach point 2 mm away from the face.
- **N60–N70** face the front face to clean the cut; X-1.0 ensures we face slightly past centre.
- **N90–N100** infeed radially to Ø36, then feed longitudinally at 0.2 mm/rev to reduce diameter.
- **N110–N120** retract radially first (avoids tool scrubbing on the shoulder), then rapid back to the start.
- **N140–N150** spindle stop and end-of-program.

---

### 3.2 Step Turning

**Workpiece:** Ø40 mm × 80 mm MS bar. Produce a three-step shaft with diameters Ø36, Ø32, Ø28 over lengths 25, 25, 20 mm respectively.

```
%
O1002 (STEP TURNING - DEMO PROGRAM)
N10  G21 G40 G99 G97
N20  G28 U0 W0
N30  T0101
N40  M03 S1400
N50  G00 X42.0 Z2.0
N60  G01 X36.0 Z-25.0 F0.2      (STEP 1: Ø36 × 25mm)
N70  G00 X42.0 Z2.0
N80  G01 X32.0 Z-50.0 F0.2      (STEP 2: Ø32 × 25mm)
N90  G00 X42.0 Z2.0
N100 G01 X28.0 Z-70.0 F0.2      (STEP 3: Ø28 × 20mm)
N110 G00 X42.0
N120 G00 Z2.0
N130 G28 U0 W0
N140 M05
N150 M30
%
```

**Logical flow:**
1. Each step is a single straight-turning block, so each step requires its **own infeed → feed → retract → return** sequence.
2. The Z-end of each step is the cumulative axial length: 25, 50, 70 mm.
3. Retract to X42 (above Ø40) to clear the workpiece before returning to Z2 — otherwise the tool will crash into the already-finished shoulder.

> [!TIP]
> **Alternative using subprogram for stepped shafts:** Write one turning cut as a subprogram and call it with G98-style repetition, varying X and Z parameters — useful for shafts with many similar steps (e.g., brake drum friction rings).

---

### 3.3 Taper Turning

**Workpiece:** Ø40 mm MS bar. Turn a taper from Ø40 to Ø30 over an axial length of 50 mm.

**Step 1 — Calculate taper half-angle:**

$$
\tan(\alpha/2) = \dfrac{40 - 30}{2 \times 50} = \dfrac{10}{100} = 0.1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \alpha/2 \approx 5.71^\circ
$$

**Step 2 — Choose cutter offset of X by (D1 − D2) and Z by L:**

```
%
O1003 (TAPER TURNING - DEMO PROGRAM)
N10  G21 G40 G99 G97
N20  G28 U0 W0
N30  T0101
N40  M03 S1400
N50  G00 X42.0 Z2.0
N60  G01 X30.0 Z-50.0 F0.15     (TAPER Ø40 → Ø30 over 50 mm)
N70  G00 X42.0
N80  G00 Z2.0
N90  G28 U0 W0
N100 M05
N110 M30
%
```

**Block N60 explanation:**
- X command = Ø30 (final small diameter).
- Z command = Z-50 (axial length of taper).
- Because G90 absolute mode and G01 modal are active, the controller interpolates a *straight-line tool path* from start point (X40, Z2) to end point (X30, Z-50).
- This line, by definition, is the tapered surface.

> [!WARNING]
> If a chamfer or radius is required at the taper end, an additional finishing pass with G01 at a shallower depth-of-cut must follow; otherwise the tool-nose radius will leave a "theoretical-error scallop" of $r(1 - \cos\theta)$ where $r$ is the insert nose radius.

---

### 3.4 Thread Cutting — Using G92 Threading Cycle

**Workpiece:** Ø20 mm finished blank. Cut an **M20 × 2.0 mm** external thread over 25 mm length.

**Step 1 — Calculate threading passes (radial in-feed schedule):**
For pitch 2.0 mm, thread depth $h_1 = 0.6134 \times 2.0 = 1.227$ mm. A typical radial infeed schedule (Fanuc convention) is to take 7–8 passes:

| Pass | Radial Infeed (mm) | X target (Ø) |
|------|--------------------|--------------|
| 1 | 0.4 | 19.6 |
| 2 | 0.3 | 19.3 |
| 3 | 0.2 | 19.1 |
| 4 | 0.15 | 18.95 |
| 5 | 0.10 | 18.85 |
| 6 | 0.05 | 18.80 |
| 7 | 0.025 | 18.775 |

**Step 2 — Spindle speed for threading:** Roughly $N \le \dfrac{1200}{P} = 600$ RPM. Take **S500**.

**Step 3 — Program using G92:**

```
%
O1004 (THREAD CUTTING - DEMO PROGRAM)
N10  G21 G40 G99 G97
N20  G28 U0 W0
N30  T0303               (60° EXTERNAL THREADING TOOL)
N40  M03 S500
N50  G00 X22.0 Z5.0      (APPROACH WITH LEAD-IN CLEARANCE)
N60  G92 X19.6 Z-25.0 F2.0     (PASS 1: Ø19.6)
N70  G00 X22.0
N80  Z5.0
N90  G92 X19.3 Z-25.0 F2.0     (PASS 2: Ø19.3)
N100 G00 X22.0
N110 Z5.0
N120 G92 X19.1 Z-25.0 F2.0
N130 G00 X22.0
N140 Z5.0
N150 G92 X18.95 Z-25.0 F2.0
N160 G00 X22.0
N170 Z5.0
N180 G92 X18.85 Z-25.0 F2.0
N190 G00 X22.0
N200 Z5.0
N210 G92 X18.80 Z-25.0 F2.0
N220 G00 X22.0
N230 Z5.0
N240 G92 X18.775 Z-25.0 F2.0    (FINISH PASS)
N250 G00 X40.0
N260 G28 U0 W0
N270 M05
N280 M30
%
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical rules for G92/G32 threading:**
> 1. The **F word must equal the thread lead** (2.0 mm). Never program the cutting feedrate.
> 2. **Spindle must be equipped with an encoder** for closed-loop synchronized cutting.
> 3. **Spindle speed override** must be disabled or held at 100 % during cutting — otherwise pitch will be wrong.
> 4. **Lead-in** distance ≥ 1 × pitch (here Z5), **lead-out** distance ≥ 1 × pitch beyond thread end.
> 5. The starting X in N50 must clear Ø22 (i.e., above Ø20) so the tool does not rub the blank on its approach.
> 6. Between each pass, **return to the same Z5 lead-in point** — otherwise the thread start will be ragged.

---

### 3.5 Circular Interpolation — G02 / G03

**Workpiece:** Ø40 mm blank. Turn a profile containing a **convex R10 fillet** between Ø40 and a Ø20 step.

**Step 1 — Identify arc parameters:**
- Start point: (X40, Z0) — at the front face.
- End point: (X20, Z-10) — 10 mm down the smaller diameter.
- Radius: R10.

> [!NOTE]
> Verify chord/midpoint radius: midpoint chord = $\sqrt{(10)^2 + (10)^2}/2 = 7.07$ mm. Sagitta $= R - \sqrt{R^2 - (L/2)^2} = 10 - \sqrt{100 - 50} = 10 - 7.07 = 2.93$ mm. Geometrically consistent.

**Step 2 — Program:**

```
%
O1005 (CIRCULAR INTERPOLATION - DEMO PROGRAM)
N10  G21 G40 G99 G97
N20  G28 U0 W0
N30  T0101
N40  M03 S1500
N50  G00 X0 Z2.0
N60  G01 Z0 F0.1
N70  G01 X20.0 F0.1          (TURN Ø20 FACING LINE)
N80  G03 X40.0 Z-10.0 R10.0  (CCW ARC R10, Ø20 → Ø40)
N90  G01 Z-50.0 F0.2         (PLAIN TURN Ø40)
N100 G00 X45.0
N110 G00 Z2.0
N120 G28 U0 W0
N130 M05
N140 M30
%
```

**Block N80 interpretation:**
- **G03** = counter-clockwise arc viewed from the **+Y direction** (looking down on the spindle axis from the tailstock toward the chuck) — this convention matches the **right-hand rule** with thumb pointing in −Z.
- **R10.0** defines the radius.
- The controller interpolates a circular path from current position (X20, Z0) to end point (X40, Z-10) with the given radius.

> [!WARNING]
> **G02 vs G03 confusion** is the single most common error in the lab. Memorise:
> - **G02 = Clockwise (CW)** as seen looking down the −Z axis toward the chuck.
> - **G03 = Counter-clockwise (CCW)**.
> For a *convex* external fillet on a turning, G03 is typical; for a *concave* internal fillet (e.g., internal shoulder relief), G02 is typical.

---

### 3.6 Combined Profile — A Typical KTU Lab Exercise

**Workpiece:** Ø40 × 60 mm MS bar. Operations in sequence:
1. Face the front face.
2. Plain turn Ø30 × 30 mm.
3. Step turn to Ø25 × 15 mm.
4. Turn a taper from Ø25 to Ø20 over 10 mm.
5. Cut an M24 × 1.5 thread over 20 mm.
6. Add a R5 convex fillet at the major thread end.

```
%
O1006 (COMBINED PROFILE - KTU LAB EXERCISE)
N10  G21 G40 G99 G97
N20  G28 U0 W0
N30  T0101
N40  M03 S1500
N50  G00 X42.0 Z2.0
N60  G01 Z0 F0.1
N70  G01 X-1.0 F0.08            (FACE FRONT FACE)
N80  G00 X42.0 Z2.0
N90  G01 X30.0 F0.2
N100 G01 Z-30.0 F0.2            (PLAIN Ø30 × 30)
N110 G01 X25.0                  (SHOULDER FACING)
N120 G01 Z-45.0 F0.2            (STEP Ø25 × 15)
N130 G01 X20.0 Z-55.0 F0.15     (TAPER Ø25 → Ø20 × 10)
N140 G02 X30.0 Z-60.0 R5.0      (R5 FILLET)
N150 G00 X42.0 Z2.0
N160 G28 U0 W0
N170 M05
N180 T0303                       (THREADING TOOL)
N190 M03 S600
N200 G00 X26.0 Z5.0
N210 G92 X23.8 Z-20.0 F1.5      (M24 × 1.5 PASS 1)
N220 X23.5
N230 X23.25
N240 X23.05
N250 X22.92
N260 X22.85
N270 X22.82                      (FINISH)
N280 G00 X40.0
N290 G28 U0 W0
N300 M05
N310 M30
%
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Thread depth check for M24 × 1.5:** $h_1 = 0.6134 \times 1.5 = 0.92$ mm. Radial infeed total = 1.18 mm (24.0 − 22.82). With 7 passes, the per-pass schedule above is reasonable. The final Ø = 24 − 1.18 ≈ 22.82 mm — matches the program.

### 3.7 Simulation Software Best Practices

**Steps to simulate before running on the real machine:**

1. **Open the simulator** (e.g., CNC Simulator Pro / FANUC CNC Guide).
2. **Define the workpiece stock** — raw diameter, length, material.
3. **Define the tool library** — tool geometry (insert nose radius, holder type).
4. **Paste the program** into the editor.
5. **Set the work zero** — usually the front face centre.
6. **Run in Single Block mode** (SBK ON) — observe each block's tool motion.
7. **Verify in 3D view** — no collisions, correct profile geometry, no over-travel into the chuck.
8. **Measure the resulting profile** with on-screen calipers — verify dimensions match the drawing.

    xmot --> xslide[Cross Slide]
    zmot --> zslide[Carriage Saddle]
    spmot --> chuck[3-Jaw Chuck + Workpiece]
    xslide --> tool[Single-Point Cutting Tool]
    zslide --> tool
    xmot -.->|Encoder Feedback| xdrv
    zmot -.->|Encoder Feedback| zdrv
    spmot -.->|Spindle Encoder| spdrv
